Lives Felt—Or Not

Reminiscences of a Viennese Psychoanalyst. By Richard F. Sterba. Detroit: Wayne State University Press, 1982, 184 pp., $39.95 hardcover.

Nathan Szajnberg

Nathan Szajnberg

3941 Duncan Place
Palo Alto, CA 94306-4551
E-mail: nmoshe@gmail.com

Nathan Szajnberg   Training and Supervising Analyst, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute and Israel Psychoanalytic Society; former Freud Professor, The Hebrew University.

To riff on a Passover tune, it would be enough had Richard Sterba given us the “therapeutic split in the ego”; it would be enough had he given us “therapeutic alliance.” But how much more Sterba gives us in the modestly titled Reminiscences of a Viennese Psychoanalyst. He offers a portrait of the German/Viennese gymnasium experience of personality Gebildeten, about which Arnold Richards (2006) has informed us: eight years of Greek, six of Latin, literature, mathematics, physics, history, zoology, botany, and chemistry. Add to that his extracurricular violin studies, which later helped him support himself. Then, after medical school, he was in the first class of students at the Vienna Institute, where he met a cavalcade of intellectual luminaries including Freud, Wilhelm Reich (before his psychosis a remarkable clinician), Paul Schilder, Margaret Mahler (his children's pediatrician before she practiced analysis; she made house calls), Karl Abraham (when he visited from Berlin), Heinz Hartmann, Robert Waelder, René Spitz, Otto Fenichel, Otto Isakower, Jeanne Lampl-de Groot, and of course Anna Freud. (Sterba had a photograph of his diploma, one of only two signed by Freud. When other candidates objected, Freud stopped signing.)

He reminds us of how apparently new ideas, such as Kohut's contrasting Tragic Man (and aggression as a reaction to developmental frustration) with Freud's Guilty Man, are often preceded by decades in earlier work: old wine in new bottles. When in 1914 Freud (in “On Narcissism”) extended “the dynamic deployment of libidinous forces into the realm of the ego” and postulated the aggressive drive as an independent force, his own colleagues had difficulty accepting his characterization of aggression as a reaction to ego frustration “caused by external … prohibitions” (p. 75).

Murray Schwartz   Professor Emeritus, Emerson College; Scholar Member and Coordinator for Multidisciplinary Psychoanalytic Studies (COMPASS), Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute.

Like the course of true love, psychoanalytic historiography never has run smooth. From the beginning, with Freud's seminal discoveries, conceptualizations of psychic life and its unconscious dimensions have been subjected to, and have informed, changing understandings of relations between selves and the social and cultural dimensions of the Western world and beyond. In both enormously generative and narrowly defensive ways, the use of psychoanalytic concepts has played consequential roles, from conservative to revolutionary, in a wide range of politically charged controversies. When in 2009 Elisabeth Young-Bruehl and Christine Dunbar constructed a timeline of psychoanalysis from 1900 to 2000, their challenge was to encompass the interplay of shifting generational, national, theoretical, institutional, social, and cultural differences and conflicts within an historiographic design that could also acknowledge major individuals and scores of important texts. The result was a foldout book replete with color-coded information on both sides, some nine feet long when the book is fully extended.

Poster Summary from the 2018 Poster Session of the American Psychoanalytic Association

Will a Video-Based Psychoanalytic Outreach Effort Engage a Young Audience? The Freudalicious Mind Project

Chris Heath

Chris Heath

7557 Rambler Road, #616
Dallas, TX 7523177
E-mail: heath1492@gmail.com

Chris Heath  

There exists a need for more effective means of psychoanalytic outreach to the community, despite psychoanalytic concepts like repression and unconscious motivation being intrinsic to the culture in the United States. People in the community are resistant to seeking psychoanalysis, believing it probably not personally helpful or available to them (IPA 2016; Zacharias Group 2002).

Moreover, there appears in particular to be a paucity of outreach efforts to youth; Outreach efforts tend to be targeted to older people, usually mental health professionals and academics, or parents and teachers.

A new common interest has developed among youth: YouTube video creators. Although some of these “YouTubers” make videos about an activity, such as a comedy act or how to do things like hair or makeup, there are many YouTube viewers who watch video bloggers. These “vloggers” simply describe their situation, their life, even such mundane experiences as what they bought at a store today (Dredge 2016). The popularity of these vloggers, who offer the appearance of chummy friendship to the viewer, suggests a hunger on the part of youth for such a connection. This population, or at least a subset of the viewership of vloggers, may be in particular need of the kind of insight and sense of wholeness that psychoanalytic treatment can provide.

Marcia Cavell   Associate Professor of Philosophy Emerita, Purchase College, State University of New York; CORST graduate, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute.

Conflict, especially unconscious intrapsychic conflict, has been defining of psychoanalysis from its inception. Indeed, several authors in this volume cite Anton Kris's remark that “psychoanalysis is human behavior from the point of view of conflict.” Psychoanalytic Perspectives on Conflict gathers together a group of highly distinguished authors to explore such questions as: Is conflict inherent in the human psyche, or does it emerge as a result of some sort of environmental failure? What are the central antagonists in the conflict? Is conflict always primary in psychopathology? And how should psychoanalytic therapy address issues of conflict?

The sixteen essays in this volume represent a wide variety of perspectives: mainstream American psychiatry, object relations, self psychology, Klein and the neo-Kleinians, attachment theory, Lacan and the French school, neuroscience, molecular genetics, cognitive psychology, and both child and adult analysis. Most of the authors are in private practice.

In the opening essay, Morris Eagle traces Freud's developing views of conflict, from his understanding of hysteria as based on ideas that are “incompatible” with consciousness, through the structural theory to the murky, late opposition of Eros versus Thanatos. What I find most provocative in this essay is Eagle's discussion of an ambiguity inherent in Freud's late aphorism, “Where id was, there ego shall be”—where it was there I shall be—suggesting, on the one hand, that in a successful therapeutic treatment the instincts will be kept in check by the ego and, on the other, that an ideal therapeutic outcome “is one in which the I (the self) is expanded to now include and integrate hitherto unintegrated aspects of oneself that had been defensively excluded …” (p. 14). This second reading, Eagle remarks, “places Freud in a broad spiritual-philosophical context in which unity of the personality is an over-riding and lifelong quest, reflected, for example, in Kierkegaard's definition of purity of heart as willing one thing …” (p. 16). Commentary on Phillips

Kathryn Zerbe

Kathryn Zerbe

1516 NW Benfield Drive
Portland, OR 97229
E-mail: kjzerbemd@comcast.net

Kathryn Zerbe  

My remarks begin with a quote from that poet, muse, and troubadour extraordinaire of the Woodstock generation—Joni Mitchell:

An angry man is an angry man.

An angry woman—bitch.

From the first days of our postgraduate psychodynamic training, we are explicitly taught and implicitly begin to understand why clinical practice actually gets harder over the course of a career. The more theories we are exposed to, the more interventions and styles we try out, and particularly after we are repeatedly tested by our most important consultants, our patients, we hone skills meant to last a lifetime only to find ourselves caught up short by how much we do not know and will never master in any given treatment process. To those just getting their sea legs in this field, such testimony might sound daunting, dreadfully depressing, jaded—a “little bitchy”—or surprisingly exciting, encouraging, even daring: You might not have to worry so much about getting it right because it's much more likely you never can, given the range of materials to draw on and from which you write your distilled, detailed process notes. So we all might reasonably wonder why it is the rare paper, such as this one by Sidney Phillips, that describes in some detail how an experienced analyst goes about making a radical departure in the middle of an ongoing analysis. Apparently both parties survive this about-face, and perhaps even begin to thrive in new ways in the treatment, because the analyst took a leap into the unknown. Philip Johnson (1906-2005) was an eminent, though in many ways controversial architectural figure in the middle and later years of the twentieth century. Of all his designs, perhaps the best known today to the general public is his Glass House, constructed in Connecticut in the late 1940s and still a popular public attraction. It is this very personal achievement

603

that the psychoanalyst Adele Tutter has taken as her base for an extensive exploration of Johnson's life and work.

Tutter addresses herself with careful detail to Johnson's early background: in particular, to the influence of a rather distant if prosperous father and an extremely attentive, dominating mother. His inevitable but stormy Harvard education was interrupted by a period of European travel, during which his acquaintance with Alfred Barr, director of the incipient New York Museum of Modern Art, led in 1930 in his appointment as chair of the Department of Architecture at that institution. Tutter also details his attraction to National Socialism, which included a fascination with Hitler and an adherence to the pro-Nazi Father Charles Coughlin, to whose blatantly anti-Semitic journal he contributed. In 1939 he traveled to Poland to witness the German seizure of Warsaw. “It was,” he wrote, “a thrilling spectacle” (p. 90). These sympathies persisted at least until American entry into World War II.

A major factor in Johnson's architectural life, Tutter emphasizes, was his intense, though often ambivalent, attachment to the great German master Mies van der Rohe. Tutter surveys in detail the mixture of this connection, parallel in many ways, dependent and resentful, with Johnson's relationship with his father. Despite Johnson's design of a major MoMA exhibition of his work, Mies was dismissive of the Glass House, and Johnson never quite forgave him. The repeated changes in his style, both among the additional buildings added to the Glass House property and the large, commercial edifices he executed in the course of his career, reflect, as Tutter convincingly shows, the successive conflictual, oedipally tinged connections he pursued over the years, not only with Mies but also with such colleagues as Frank Lloyd Wright, Le Corbusier, Eero Saarinen, and Frank Gehry.

Situated in the vast literature of the last two decades on the intergenerational transmission of trauma, Roger Frie's Not in My Family stands out

605

as both a courageous, moral act and an important contribution to our understanding of the process of such transmission. Rich in insight, the book is the result of a painful and honest exploration of his family legacy of his maternal grandfather's having been an active Nazi. The book has deservedly received the Canadian Jewish Literary Award for History (the Canadian Jewish Literary Awards recognize the finest books with Jewish themes and subjects by Canadian authors in a variety of genres). Frie recounts a difficult and long psychological journey. Growing up in Canada the son of German emigrés, he had learned about the Holocaust from his parents, but, as he writes, “to safeguard my identification with my grandparents I dissociated threatening images and thoughts about them. I avoided the difficult questions in an environment in which questions were not generally asked” (p. 55). “My knowledge about my grandfather was … linked to what was talked about and what was cloaked in silence, to the questions that were asked and not asked… . I maintained a stance of ‘not knowing’ by dissociating threatening images of who my grandfather may have been. But this dissociative stance was only possible because I already ‘knew’ in some sense that my grandfather could have been involved in the Nazi past. My stance of simultaneous knowing and not knowing was hardly unique. In fact, it is a good illustration of the collective German struggle with memory in the wake of the Holocaust” (p. 179). Such was Frie's psychological situation at the beginning of his journey. He was able to perceive affects surrounding topics he accurately sensed were things he should not ask about or know, though from his general knowledge he had some idea of what those things were.

One of the virtues of this book is the clarity with which Frie elucidates the means by which intergenerational transmission of the experience of his grandfather's shameful and guilty actions was effected. Eschewing occult ideas of unconscious mechanisms whereby traumatic experiences are affectively transmitted wholesale in a fashion almost Lamarkian, he specifies the political, social, and historical contexts that determine the available facts and personal encounters with family that provide the material from which later generations construct their understanding of the traumatic experiences of their forebears.

Some time ago I suggested to JAPA's book review editors, Rosemary Balsam and Paul Schwaber, that they consider publishing a second review of this book, first published in English in 2013, not at all because the

999

earlier review, by Rogelio Sosnik (2011), was lacking. Indeed, having reread his review before writing this—thinking that a second review might not add anything—I was impressed with his excellent summary of the book and his apt plea for anglophone readers to familiarize themselves with the work of José Bleger. I share the hope that analysts lacking access to the South American literature available only in Spanish will seek out and be edified by this English translation of Bleger's work. But Sosnik's review was published two years before the book appeared at long last in English, after an earlier translation, never completed and never published, had been in the works for over twenty years. While Sosnik rightly celebrated the “fact” that it was finally here, alas, it languished those two years before seeing the light of day, only, to my knowledge, to promptly sink without a trace.

So here is another plea for this book to be more widely known. And in the process of stating a case for more of us English-speaking psychoanalysts and analytic therapists to come to know it, I want to widen the context in which we might see it by substituting some of my own language for the language Bleger inherited from his sources, particularly two crucial terms that to my mind obscure his meaning, at least for anglophone readers. I will get to those terms later.

The one paper of note that Bleger published in English appeared in 1967, the same year this book saw its original publication in Spanish. The paper is a fairly well-known contribution on the analytic frame. The book's translators have used “setting” instead of “frame,” a better rendering of Bleger's Spanish word encuadre. Bleger holds that the setting should always be analyzed, as it is there that psychotic aspects of the personality reveal themselves and, in addition, that transference to the totality of the analysis appears. The setting is thus a representation of the newborn's foundational experience.

Flectere si nequeo superos, Acheronta movebo. Every psychoanalyst should instantly recognize this inscription, not only for its place in psychoanalysis but also from its original context in Virgil's Aeneid. Alas, I highly doubt the latter, and Freud's use of the line as an epigraph to the dream book is a fantasy of white Western privilege. Similarly, I think the bulk of Joseph Fernando's book presents concepts all psychoanalysts should be familiar with: drives, repression, defense, reconstruction, and more. Yet many psychoanalytic fundaments have been declared moribund of late, and are discarded or lost: forgotten, suppressed, or repressed by the counterforce of uncomfortable inconvenience and theoretical reformation. Today's study and use of the psychoanalytic past is paradoxically reclaimed in outsider circles as a chic “return to Freud,” unwittingly tweaking the Yom Kippur liturgy of repentance and renewal via a return to faith, though leavened in the current instance with a healthy skepticism. The ubiquity of loss and the awkward conflict over return permeate psychoanalytic practice, so that we might expect a coherent practice and theory. Instead we find ourselves split, splintered, and reassembled with philosophical duct tape and bubble gum. Ideas of the mind as a system of dynamic functions mediating and translating interfaces at the frontiers of various realities and traumas, including losing or retaining experiences of self and other, have been superseded by ideological camps on one or the other side of the psychic-somatic and self-social divides, claiming either integration or the other's irrelevance.

Anyone who thinks that psychoanalysis is old hat and that it has been eclipsed by modern neuroscience should read Antonio Damasio's latest book, The Strange Order of Things, and think again. If something deeply true about an aspect of nature is discovered from the viewpoint of one discipline, then it should perhaps not surprise us if—given time—the same truth should be stumbled upon afresh by another discipline. This is what seems to have happened with our understanding of the biological underpinnings of human culture.

The Strange Order of Things reads as a neuroscientific vindication of Freud's views on the topic, as set out in his Civilization and Its Discontents (Freud 1930) and “Why War?” (Einstein and Freud 1933)—albeit with many revisions and updated details, and slightly less pessimism about the implications for humanity. Damasio even goes so far as to conclude, in effect, that our one hope for overcoming the destructive influence of the drives upon human culture is something akin to psychoanalysis. “Feelings,” he writes, “freed humans to attempt homeostasis by cultural means, instead of remaining captive to their basic biological devices… . Their job was never finished. A life not felt would need no cure. A life felt but not examined would not have been curable” (pp. 232-233).

In short, in this book, Damasio (like Freud before him) arrives at the view that civilization and culture1 are at bottom elaborations of a deeply conservative biological force. Whereas Damasio calls this force homeostasis,

After a performance of Mozart's Jupiter Symphony led by Leonard Bernstein, a listener approached the maestro and asked, “What is it that Mozart puts into his music that makes it so powerful?” Bernstein is said to have replied, “Dear boy, it's not what he puts in, it's what he leaves out.” Ellen Pinsky's Death and Fallibility in the Psychoanalytic Encounter is built around an understanding of what is missing—of loss, transience, and vulnerability, particularly in relation to evocativeness and ethical responsibility. In this short collection of essays, Pinsky's extraordinary power as a writer mirrors these themes. Her book is designed to evoke powerful responses in the reader, by the magic of words, both her own and those borrowed from novelists, playwrights, and poets. Pinsky avoids comprehensive, systematic exploration of core subjects central to her arguments (e.g., countertransference, enactment, abstinence, neutrality, and boundary violation), all of which is available elsewhere. When needed, each concept is illustrated clinically and connected to its theoretical origins but is not, in keeping with her essay format, buried in a torrent of scholarly historical exposition. Pinsky aims instead to move her reader, and her discipline, to both scold and valorize. I felt moved to do both, in addition to feeling enormous admiration for her having captured the majesty and audacity of the psychoanalytic enterprise.

Pinsky's essays are organized around two interrelated clinical domains that propel her argument. On the one hand, the analyst may fail the patient via his mortality and human fallibility. On the other, the psychoanalytic situation, as originated by Freud, invites the patient's passionate attachment and desire to be met only with verbal exchange.

The title of Irvin Yalom's memoir, Becoming Myself—his latest and probably last book he tells us, writing at the age of eighty-five, still active and doing therapy—arouses our curiosity, because in the most obvious sense I cannot become myself since I am, inevitably and always, myself. We find clues to his meaning in his absorbing and well-researched novel, When Nietzsche Wept (1992), a fictional account of Nietzsche's relationships with Freud and his mentor, Josef Breuer, in which Yalom has Nietzsche say to Breuer: “It is not the truth that is holy, but the search for one's own truth. Can there be a more sacred act than self-inquiry? … one of my granite sentences is ‘Become who you are’” (p. 68). And later: “Nothing must interfere with the development of the hero inside of you… . You want to fly, but you cannot begin to fly by flying. I must teach you how to walk, and the first step in learning to walk is to understand that he who does not obey himself is ruled by others” (pp. 176-177).

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz  

It is a truth universally acknowledged that we now practice psychoanalysis in a context of multiple, even competing theories, with a multiplicity of attendant techniques. A recent APsaA panel (Zimmer 2017) explored this fact, offering several possible resolutions for practicing analysts to consider: choose one theory and stick with it (Blass 2017); allow multiple perspectives to add nuance to one's personal core theory (Cooper 2017); incorporate aspects of different perspectives as one's personal core theory continues to evolve (LaFarge 2017).

It is difficult to imagine an analyst being trained in America today who has not been exposed to multiple theoretical perspectives; or to believe that an analyst, thus educated and having undergone a personal analysis, would not have developed a preference among approaches to treatment—in other words, a personal core theory of mind, including its developmental needs and the analyst's potential role in facilitating a patient's growth.

In his clinical plenary Sidney Phillips presents us a detailed example of how one analyst works with techniques from different theoretical perspectives. Phillips's moving account describes his decision to shift, during the course of an ongoing analysis, from his chosen personal core theory of object relations, with its technique of interpretation and overcoming resistance, to an alternative approach—self psychological empathic responsiveness. Phillips presents this change in technique as a needed preparatory phase in the treatment, hopefully enabling the engagement he had originally sought with the patient as two individuals in a self and object relationship.

The relevance of understanding the basis on which we make our clinical decisions is, arguably, even more critical to our work today than it was for Freud, who continued to ponder questions of evidence until the end of his life. When he set forth the beginnings of our discipline, he presented a single technique.

25 Main Street
Stockbridge, MA 01262
E-mail: jane.tillman@austenriggs.net

Jane Tillman  

suicide, disillusion, illusion, development

Both illusion and disillusion play an important role in development, fostering processes of going-on-being and separation (Winnicott 1960). The capacity to bear disillusionment is both a developmental necessity and an ongoing challenge. Disillusionment penetrates the sphere of illusion and invites the individual into an expanded encounter with shared realities. But when disillusionment becomes chronic and pervasive or is accompanied by severe psychic pain, then suicide is felt by some to be an urgent option for refusing or escaping this pain. Contextualized within a review of psychoanalytic developmental theories of disillusion, vignettes from a research study of participants who have survived a near lethal suicide attempt illuminate the phenomenon of suicide as an illusory solution to what feels like unbearable disillusionment.

Alan Sugarman  

Newell Fischer's brief note on a time-limited psychotherapy with an eighty-year-old patient—a man suffering from a recurrent nightmare for over a decade—is intriguing given the many interesting questions it raises. In fact, it can serve almost as a Rorschach card, offering as it does inroads for exploration of whatever areas of interest a reader might have. Just as Fischer has a clear interest in preverbal memory, reconstruction, and trauma that guides his thought here, so my interest in conflict theory, development, pathogenesis, and mutative action will inform my comments. That they differ from the author's conclusions should not be taken as a criticism. Rather, I am using his clearly interesting and apparently helpful treatment as an opportunity to comment on long-held interests of mine. From that perspective, my comments are meant to be heuristic and to highlight views from a different theoretical perspective.

In essence, Fischer suggests that the patient's interesting nightmare reflects a pre-representational memory of cumulative trauma suffered during his breast feeding experiences. Clinical theories of trauma such as this one carry an implicit formulation different from more Freudian theories of trauma (see, e.g., Busch 2005; Sugarman 2008). “Clinical models formulated on this basis assume that real object relationships are stored in non-declarative implicit memory as ‘implicit’ or ‘procedural’ memories … or ‘implicit memory-objects’” (Bohleber 2007, p. 334).

Anne Erreich

170 West End Avenue, Suite 1S
New York, NY 10023

Anne Erreich  

infant mentation, development, linguistics, traumatic memory, priming, philosophy of mind, attachment, interdisciplinary

How should psychoanalysis situate itself with regard to disciplines in the academy? Some have argued that psychoanalysis is a unique discipline whose theory and methodology will derive little or nothing from other disciplines (Green 2000), while others have looked outward to other disciplines and attempted to integrate their findings with accepted psychoanalytic knowledge (Solms 1995, 2013; D.N. Stern 2000). The prevalence of psychoanalytic papers related especially to the arts and humanities gives the appearance of robust interdisciplinary work on the part of psychoanalysts. In this regard, it may be useful to distinguish between interdisciplinary work and applied psychoanalysis.

Interdisciplinary work in the academic world happens naturally, as scholars regularly attend university presentations in adjoining fields for the betterment of their own. Scholars look to neighboring disciplines to find data to support or disconfirm their hypotheses, to provide metaphors for new hypotheses, and to generally enrich their thinking about their home discipline.

I am honored to be part of this panel created in the memory of Richard Gottlieb. Given the panel's purpose, I had to decide how to approach his contribution to it (Gottlieb 2017). The best way, it seemed to me, is to react to the paper just as I would if he were living. I will be straightforward in disagreeing with Gottlieb when that's how I feel, and I will be using the paper, and the topic of historical reconstruction in general, as a springboard for my own perspective on the subject.

I was surprised to read the first sentence of Gottlieb's abtract: “The psychoanalytic process of reconstruction has yet to be reexamined from the perspective of today's two-person psychologies.” This statement certainly does not square with my reading of the literature. Gottlieb himself cites Edgar Levenson's work, in which the story of the patient's life that comes into being in the treatment is understood to be shaped in part by the analytic relatedness, especially its unconscious aspects—just as the relatedness, of course, is significantly shaped by the history. But Levenson is hardly alone.

Poetry

Climate of Opinion: Sigmund Freud in Poetry. Edited by Irene Willis. New York: International Psychoanalytic Books, 2017, xvi + 206 pp., $19.95 paperback.

Victor Altshul

Victor Altshul

400 Prospect Street
New Haven, CT 06511
E-mail: victor.altshul@yale.edu

Victor Altshul   Associate Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Yale University School of Medicine.

The poet, writer, and educator Irene Willis has published an intriguing book of poems that derive from or refer to psychoanalysis. Her original intent was to solicit from a variety of poets poems that make explicit reference to Freud. Her purview expanded when she began receiving poetry that did not explicitly mention Freud but that concerned itself with psychoanalysis generally and individual analyses in particular. The results are varied, often quite original, and generally fresh and engaging.

The book takes its title from W. H. Auden's “In Memory of Sigmund Freud.” In her introduction Willis quotes the relevant passage:

For one who'd lived among enemies so long:

If often he was wrong, and, at times, absurd,

To us he is no more a person

Now, but a whole climate of opinion

This well-known and well-crafted homage to Freud, the first poem in the book, is followed by Hilda Doolittle's famous poem “The Master,” a lively tribute to her analyst that is in part encomium, in part a peevish scolding for what she experienced as his unrelenting sexism—an accusation, she persuades us, that is not without merit.

These extraordinary poems are followed by two by Anna Freud, one written as a young girl, of lesser power but of biographical and historical interest. I'm puzzled by the inclusion of Anne Carson's “Freud Town,” intended, I take it, to describe the terrors of the unconscious; and I would have chosen Philip Larkin's brilliant “Aubade” or “Sad Steps” over his bellicosely ironic “This Be the Verse.”

Fortunately poems of exquisite poignancy vastly outnumber lesser ones.

Après-coup. By Jean Laplanche. Translated by Jonathan House and Luke Thurston. New York: The Unconscious in Translation, 2017, xviii + 253 pp., $55.00 hardcover, $39.50 paperback.

Deborah Browning

Deborah Browning

29 East 9th Street, #4
New York, NY 10003
E-mail: deborah.browning@nyu.edu

Deborah Browning   Adjunct Associate Professor, Department of Psychology, New York University Graduate School of Arts and Science (retired); member, American Psychoanalytic Association.

Freud's theory of childhood seduction trauma went through many iterations both during his own lifetime of thought and afterwards, as its deferred impact on psychoanalytic theory and practice stimulated much retrospective reconsideration and revision. The present book, Après-coup, a collection of lectures and essays by Jean Laplanche, provides, through the lens of time, a way back to the study of the evolution of Freud's theories of seduction and trauma and then a way forward to Laplanche's own general theory of seduction and the recurrently traumatic impact of the enigmatic message. This is the most recent production of the American press, The Unconscious in Translation, which has tasked itself with the eventual translation of Laplanche's entire oeuvre into English. Considering this an important project (full disclosure), I was glad to copy edit at no charge their recent translation of New Foundations for Psychoanalysis; hence I am acknowledged in a general way in this book, although I had no part in the present production. The main work in this volume is the first English translation of Laplanche's Problematiques VI, L'après-coup (2006), his own written presentation of a nine-lecture series given at the University of Paris between November 1989 and February 1990. Directly related to a psychoanalytic theory of time, the lectures focus on Freud's Nachträglichkeit—both the word and the concept—in all its ambiguity and incompleteness. Après-coup has become, as I will clarify below, the French translation of Nachträglichkeit, while deferred action was Strachey's choice for the Standard Edition—a decision criticized by many.

[[RunningHead]]

Comings and Goings

As we begin another year and another JAPA volume, we must bid farewell to the following Editorial Board members who are now rotating off the board: Leon Balter, Heather Berlin, Morris Eagle, Michael Garfinkle, Sybil Houlding, Claudia Lament, Judith Yanof, and Lynne Zeavin. We are fortunate to have Morris Eagle remain with JAPA in a different capacity, adding his broad expertise to our Advisory Board. Also leaving the board at this time are JAPA Editorial Associates Eileen Becker-Dunn, Israel Katz, and Gavin Mullen.

Our board members' commitment to the highest standards of the peer-review process sustains JAPA's place as a valued source of psychoanalytic scholarship. We are grateful for their efforts, as are our submitting authors, who regularly acknowledge to us the benefits of our board's constructive input to their work.

We wish to welcome the following new Editorial Board members for the term running from January 1, 2018, through December 31, 2020: Daria Colombo, Henry J. Friedman, Axel Hoffer, Kay Long, Kerry Kelly Novick, Eslee Samberg, Michael Shulman, and Alan Sugarman. We also look forward to working over the next two years with three new Editorial Associates: Leslie Keith, Michael Levin, and Gregory Rizzolo.

Sadly, our longtime Managing Editor, Marilyn Herleth, was forced to leave us due to illness. We will miss her no-nonsense approach and wry sense of humor, familiar to generations of JAPA board members and authors. We are fortunate to have found Jennifer Mahar, our new Managing Editor, who brings to JAPA a wealth of experience in journal publishing.

These are times of major changes within American psychoanalysis, as clinical practices and institutional guidelines continue to evolve. These changes within our psychoanalytic profession are taking place within a larger context of major cultural shifts, due to transformations in the creation, dissemination, and accessibility of information. No aspect of our lives, including scholarly journals, will be untouched by this digital tsunami. It is thus more important than ever that we at JAPA hold fast to the lodestar of true scholarship, which consists of thoughtful and fair peer review, constructive dialogue with authors, and careful editing of manuscripts. These goals remain our commitment to our readers.

“The world's most famous filing cabinet,” an artifact now housed in the Smithsonian Museum of American History, once belonged to the Los Angeles psychoanalyst Lewis Fielding. The cabinet shows obvious damage from having been pried open on September 3, 1971, by President Richard Nixon's “Plumbers” in their first foray into illegal spying on fellow citizens. The Plumbers sought information about any knowledge that Daniel Ellsberg, one of Fielding's patients, might have of Nixon's secret plans for expanding the war in Vietnam. The Plumbers may have wanted to “neutralize Ellsberg.” When this matter came to public attention during Ellsberg's trial for having released the classified “Pentagon Papers,” the media reported that the Plumbers had found nothing. On the contrary: they had discovered Ellsberg's file in the cabinet. They saw in that file a copy of a paper Ellsberg had written indicating that he had indeed seen relevant classified information (Edwards 2012).

Terrible as this break-in was, and despite the fact that it unleashed a chain of events leading to Watergate and ultimately to Nixon's resignation three years later, the incident now seems quaint to us because the means used to access Ellsberg's file was a mere crowbar, hardly the kind of high-tech tool with which such intrusions are carried out today. By 1991, tales in which analysts or their patients were intruded upon could still be the stuff of comedies like that year's What about Bob?, in which Bill Murray plays a highly dependent patient who stalks his new analyst (Richard Dreyfuss) on his New Hampshire vacation. In the transformed world we work in today, however, reality has taken a darker turn, not only in the lives of psychoanalysts and their patients, but in virtually everyone's life.

If the lilting title Stalker, Hacker, Voyeur, Spy reminds readers of John le Carré's Tinker, Tailor, Soldier, Spy, the reference is deliberate, as Helen Gediman readily acknowledges in her preface. She rejected her original title, Stalker, Hacker, Bugger, Spy,

Alfred Margulies

Alfred Margulies

1493 Cambridge Street
Cambridge, MA 02139
E-mail: alfred_margulies@hms.harvard.edu

Alfred Margulies  

disillusionment, illusionment, foundational illusion, uncanny, après-coup, Nachträglichkeit, being-in-the-world, speech acts, symbolic order, imagined realities

One can only be disillusioned if once one lived within illusions—and so disillusionment is always after the fact (après coup or nachträglich), as illusions come into view even as they are crumbling within. With a crisis of disillusionment—or existential disillusionment—one falls away from a coherence of meaning, revealing a system of intertwined fundamental illusions that had always been lived within and implicit, part of one's being-in-the-world, and that now seem broken, strange, and uncanny. This way of being that one recognizes only retrospectively may be called “illusionment,” a state of being apprehended in the very process of its falling apart. That is, prior to disillusionment, there may not have been an illusion as such; rather, there was some overall effective-enough, taken-for-granted coherence, an experience of world and being that now comes into view and seems broken precisely because it no longer holds together. For example, a sense of a common “we” might be revealed to be so flawed as to have been a fiction, a fantasy, a set of pervasive, interwoven illusions that one had once naively lived within as unquestioned beliefs. Severe disillusionment, then, carries with it a sense of falling out of the once taken-for-granted world, undermining a sense of solid existential grounding. Saturating all aspects of the psyche, traumatizing disillusionments may lead to a lifelong cascade of après coup attempts to find new grounding in how to live, as one attempts to repair this world-broken-ness.

Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Institute; faculty, Harvard Medical School, Department of Psychiatry, the Cambridge Health Alliance.

220 Fifth Avenue, Suite 1107

Orly Marcovich   Private practice of psychoanalysis.

Juan Gennaro's new book, In the Defiles of the Psyche, is an extraordinary, at times breathtaking, anthology of clinical cases, the product of a psychoanalytic work life, in which the reader witnesses the encounter between Gennaro and his patients.

819

The book's title is an apt, polysemic metaphor for the encounter between Gennaro and his patients. A defile is a narrow passage, an intermediate space, a gap, here between analyst and patient. A defile is a narrow canyon, a gorge, a trail between walls allowing “transferential movements.”

The first part of the book, “First Steps: In the Defiles of the Abyss,” a psychoanalytic reflection on the fundamental causes and dramatic scenarios in which psychosis develops, presents in detail the clinical cases of seven psychotic patients at l'Eau Vive, a French psychiatric hospital. Influenced by Gisela Pankow and D. W. Winnicott, and aiming to repair his patients’ fragmented selves, Gennaro invites them to model with clay, thus creating a transitional creative space. Modeling with clay achieves at least two goals: on one hand, it presents a dimension of reality, a concrete space, while on the other it suggests the disorganized internal world of the patient, governed principally by the primary process.

The second part of the book, “On the Pathway,” is a detailed presentation of six clinical cases. (Gennaro had at this point left l'Eau Vive.) The titles of these clinical cases gives the reader an idea of Gennaro's incisive interest in his clinical work, and of his intellectual thoroughness in exploring theoretical conceptualizations. Here are four: “Fragmentation and Integration of the Oedipal Conflict”; “Reflections on Paternity and the Wish to Be a Father”; “A Case of Lasthénie de Ferjol Syndrome”; and “Play in Psychoanalysis with Children.”

While Gennaro's notion of transference points to Freud's second topography, it also includes some contemporary psychoanalytic approaches. It is this consolidation of ideas from classical and contemporary psychoanalytic theory that defines Latin American psychoanalysis today.

A Conversation with Adam Phillips

David Fisher

David Fisher

1800 Fairburn Avenue, Suite 203
Los Angeles, CA 90025
E-mail: djamesfisherphd@gmail.com

David Fisher  

David James Fisher: It's my privilege to introduce Adam Phillips as NCP's first Master Clinician in Residence. This program is designed for our institute to interact with someone who has produced a significant body of psychoanalytic writing. Phillips's books and essays are grounded dialectically in clinical knowledge and a subtle understanding of the therapeutic process. His visit will present him an opportunity to be immersed in the life of our institute, exchanging ideas with our candidates, members, and faculty.

What can I say about Adam Phillips? To begin with, he doesn't have e-mail. [Laughter] I am of two minds about this. On the one hand, I find it charming and on the other hand, a pain in the neck. Perhaps it is a critique of technology, a protest against the age of social media, of overly rapid e-mailing and texting, and of facile forms of relating that are actually forms of non-relating. More significantly, Phillips is adept at communicating and non-communicating. He has a special quality of relating intimately, of listening and being attuned; he then can courteously withdraw, retreating into a much cherished solitude.

Mark Twain once quipped that England and America were two countries separated by the same language. Phillips represents much of the best in British intellectual and psychoanalytic life. Our own tradition, blending classical and contemporary psychoanalysis, will hopefully form an intermediate and transitional space between our different psychoanalytic languages, attitudes, and methods.

Adam Phillips is a distinguished and productive writer. He has written twenty-one books and edited eight others.

David James Fisher, Training and Supervising Analyst, Institute of Contemporary Psychoanalysis; Senior Faculty Member, New Center for Psychoanalysis.

Teaching Psychotherapy to Psychoanalytic Candidates

Sabrina Cherry , Michele Rosenberg and Eve Caligor

Eve Caligor

Sabrina Cherry
285 Central Park West
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: sc26@cumc.columbia.edu

Eve Caligor  

psychotherapy, psychoanalytic training

Psychoanalytic institutes have developed a variety of approaches to address the reality that psychoanalytically trained clinicians generally practice more psychodynamic psychotherapy than they do formal psychoanalysis. At the Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research we developed a course for advanced candidates challenging them to integrate what they have learned about doing psychoanalysis during training with their ongoing fund of knowledge about psychotherapy practice. We encourage them to consider how they select treatments and to reflect on similarities and differences between the two modalities with regard to listening, selecting a focus, intervening, and managing the relationship. We also discuss how they approach terminations and how they transition between psychoanalysis and psychotherapy. We selectively use the psychotherapy research literature grounded in the common factors approach in order to update candidates about current knowledge in the field.

Discussion: Reconstruction in Relation to Therapeutic Action

Andrew Rosendahl

Andrew Rosendahl

124 West 79th Street
New York, NY 10024

Andrew Rosendahl  

It is my pleasure to be part of this forum in memory of Richard Gottlieb. I am fortunate to have known him as both teacher and mentor. I was always impressed by Richard's erudition and enthusiasm about all things psychoanalytic; but most of all, I remember his kind, inquisitive, and curious manner with me and with others.

And so it is fitting that with his paper on reconstruction and with this panel, Richard has opened a door for us to become curious again, not just about reconstruction, but about the very core of psychoanalysis: the mechanism of its therapeutic action.

I want to thank Leon Hoffman for putting this panel together and also to thank our panelists: Harold Blum, Donnel Stern, and Richard Lane. We are fortunate to have this esteemed group share with us a wide range of ideas about reconstruction and therapeutic change.

In fact, it is apparently hard to speak of reconstruction without talking about therapeutic action. We can see this reflected in Gottlieb's last paper (2017), as well as in our panelists, each of whom has spoken as much about the mutative process in psychoanalysis as of reconstructing the past. I think this is in part because Freud tied historical reconstruction to the curative process of psychoanalysis. But perhaps even more influential is the fact that reconstruction involves memory, emotion, and experience, three elements we hear a lot about in these papers and that play a critical role in most any model of therapeutic action.

On my first reading of our panelists’ contributions, these works seemed somewhat at odds, but as I digested them I found the ideas to be ultimately consistent, even complementary.

Faculty, New York Psychoanalytic Institute.

[[RunningHead]]

“Of Construction, or, if it is Preferred, of Reconstruction”

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz

E-mail: bonlit@gmail.com

Bonnie Litowitz  

Freud's last papers, published shortly before his death, intrigue us for the double perspective they afford. On the one hand, they offer an opportunity to share his retrospective evaluation of his whole “project,” as in “Analysis Terminable and Interminable” (1937a) or “An Outline of Psycho-Analysis” (1940a). On the other hand, we also look to them for clues to what Freud thought needed continued scrutiny—for what future post-Freudian “projects” should address. As we read these late papers we find ourselves as he did at the start of “Splitting of the Ego in the Process of Defence” (1940b): “I find myself for a moment in the interesting position of not knowing whether what I have to say should be regarded as something long familiar and obvious or as something entirely new and puzzling” (p. 275).1

One of these last papers, “Constructions in Analysis” (1937b), from which my title is taken, presents that double perspective, looking back but also raising questions for us today. Here (as in “Analysis Terminable and Interminable”) Freud is addressing the question of evidence: on what basis do we justify our technique? Freud begins the paper by invoking a favorite analogy, comparing the work of analysts to that of archaeologists. Our task, like theirs, “is to make out what has been forgotten from the traces which it has left behind, or more correctly, to construct it… . [The task is] of construction, or, if it is preferred, of reconstruction… . Both [analyst and archaeologist] have an undisputed right to reconstruct by means of supplementing and combining the surviving remains” (1937b, pp. 258-259). In looking back over his writings, Freud then considers how he has dealt with this topic:

If, in accounts of analytic technique, so little is said about ‘constructions’, that is because ‘interpretations’ and their effects are spoken of instead.

1He was “inclined to think the latter” (Freud 1940b, p. 275).83

Whenever a group of analysts share in the common task of considering an author's work—as is the case with this volume—the editors can never be sure whether the invited commentators will stick to the task at hand or take the opportunity to speak about vaguely related matters that largely take leave of the considered work. This brings to mind the parable of the elephant and the flea—relayed to me by Joe Lichtenberg at a recent ApsaA meeting—which aptly captures what sometimes happens after a paper is delivered and the analyst chosen to comment delivers a discussion that fails to grapple with the author's chief arguments:

A biologist was asked to discuss a paper about elephants. His area of expertise is insects, not mammals. He began: “The elephant is a very large animal with a long trunk and floppy ears. He is sometimes irritatingly beset upon by fleas. Now fleas are a small insect that …”

This edited volume contains the reactions of ten well-chosen, theoretically and geographically diverse analysts, all of them highly knowledgeable about the subject matter: Freud's remarkably brief foundational paper “Formulations on the Two Principles of Mental Functioning.” As one might expect, some of the contributors have written chapters that touch only tangentially on Freud's paper on their way to addressing what chiefly interests them (their “fleas”). I'm not saying these chapters aren't well-written and worth reading; rather, I am simply noting the regularity of such occurrences, which typically plague efforts of this sort.

The book is masterfully edited by Gabriela Legorreta and Lawrence J.

This remarkable book, published ten years ago, has gone through several editions, selling in the neighborhood of fifty thousand copies. This is an unusual phenomenon in the Peruvian book market.

This extraordinary achievement has been possible because Jorge Bruce is a sui generis psychoanalyst: besides working with patients on the couch, he writes a weekly column in one of the country's most prestigious newspapers about the political and social conflicts in Peruvian life. In that column he clearly evinces a psychoanalytic perspective.

Thus, this book combines the perspective of the columnist with that of the psychoanalyst, providing us an illustrated account of prejudice and racism, realities evident in all aspects of Peruvian life. This book has not been written exclusively for psychoanalysts; rather, it aims to engage as many people as possible who are interested in the subject of racism in Peruvian society.

I will say at the outset that this little book is a gem. When asked to review the book, I thought it a good opportunity to revisit Melanie Klein's work. In a way, I had begun the most important paths of my own analytic identity with a simultaneous immersion in Klein and her contemporary, Ronald Fairbairn. Exposure to their writings drew me from America to London, where I found ready access to dyed-in-the-wool Kleinians at the Tavistock Clinic in the early 1970s, but had to ferret out help with Fairbairn because John Sutherland had retired from his post as medical director of the Tavistock and returned to Scotland. Klein was in the air and the water then, just as Anna Freud was the soul of what was then the Hampstead Clinic.

Klein had died twelve years before I arrived in 1972, and Bion was not yet recognized. Although his formative papers had been written, he was hardly mentioned, even by those who later become his disciples. Winnicott had died two years earlier, and a strong but different contingent of Tavistock faculty taught his work.

So the atmosphere in which I received Klein's teaching was reverential, giving the feeling that one had missed the golden days by being just a bit too young and arriving too late on the scene. And Mrs. Klein was presented as a towering and formidable figure, to be revered and feared, her exploration of the primitive inner world a veritable Walpurgisnacht, a celebration of human destructiveness that lay at the heart of darkness in each of us.

Madelon Sprengnether   Regents Professor Emerita, Department of English, University of Minnesota.

Moments when the original poet in each of us created the outside world for us, by finding the familiar in the unfamiliar, are perhaps forgotten by most people; or else they are guarded in some secret place of memory because they were too much like visitations of the gods to be mixed with everyday thinking.

—Marion Milner (1952, p. 88)

Thinking about creativity and its enigmatic sources inevitably invokes the figure of the artist's muse. As Adele Tutter, editor of The Muse, reminds us, the nine muses (each responsible for a different art form) were conceived as the offspring of gods: Zeus/Jupiter and Mnemosyne. While Zeus commanded all of the gods, Mnemosyne embodied the specific functions of memory. Such a distinguished lineage must have expressed the ancients' reverence for creativity, since they regarded its provenance as divine.

While we can imagine Zeus as a figure of supreme power and might, Mnemosyne seems more human, as she represents a capacity that we all rely on in the course of our everyday lives. In this sense, Mnemosyne seems closer to Marion Milner's notion of the “original poet in each of us” than to the gods of the Greco-Roman pantheon.

Tutter's rich collection of essays helps us enter the lives and worlds of extraordinary people whose inner lives, due to the clarity and insightfulness of the authors' analyses, seem not so different from our own. The interpretations they offer are enriched by their deep knowledge of their subjects, rendered accessible to lay readers through their integration of art history, biography, literary criticism, and psychoanalytic theory into clear narrative prose.

John Terry Maltsberger (1933-2016) was an American psychoanalyst who greatly influenced studies of the suicidal patient, and a suicidologist whose contributions significantly impacted psychoanalysis. Through his devotion to the understanding and treatment of suicidal people he exerted a major influence in both areas. Throughout a long and productive career, Maltsberger focused on an uncomfortable area of the psyche, that sphere that impels the attack on the self. His position in psychoanalysis stands out for his early emphasis on the patient's internal subjective experience and the dynamics of the therapeutic engagement. He had a broad range of knowledge and interests beyond psychoanalysis and was able to integrate perspectives from empirical studies with his empathic understanding of clinical material and a striking ability to make complex and impenetrable intrapsychic processes lucidly understandable.

Rex McGehee   Training and Supervising Analyst, Child, Adolescent, and Adult, Denver Institute for Psychoanalysis; Associate Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University of Colorado School of Medicine.

With the publication of Manual of Regulation-Focused Psychotherapy for Children with Externalizing Behaviors: A Psychodynamic Approach, Leon Hoffman, Timothy Rice, and Tracy Prout have given us something to think about. They have written a treatment manual accessible to most therapists for a short-term, psychodynamically derived therapy (RFP-C) to help children struggling with externalizing behaviors. As a child analyst practicing for thirty years, I can read this book only through the eyes of my experience; doubtless, then, this essay will convey the bias with which I have approached the treatment of such children. I will first describe what the authors have attempted in this manual, after which I will discuss what I found most useful in their approach and what might be problematic in it.

The book begins with a foreword by Nick Midgley. After placing the manual in context, Midgley notes that RFP-C is designed for the most common referral for outpatient treatment of children, that of externalizing disorders. This would include diagnoses such as Attention Deficit Disorder, Oppositional Defiant Disorder, Conduct Disorder, and the more recently described Dysregulation Disorder. These disorders, understood to be heterogeneous, are often difficult to treat. These children are understood as having “deficits in implicit emotional regulation” (p. xviii), doubtless the result of both genetic and environmental factors. What makes this manual unusual is its insistence that externalizing behaviors are not just to be managed, but to be understood. Symptom resolution will require attending to the function and meaning of the symptom.

Nancy Kulish   Training and Supervising Analyst, Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute; Adjunct Professor, Department of Psychiatry, School of Medicine at Wayne State University; Adjunct Professor of Psychology, University of Detroit/Mercy.

This book, edited by Paula Ellman and Nancy Goodman, begins with the statement “Psychoanalysis views unconscious fantasy as foundational in the understanding of psychic realities” (p. 1). The editors’ intent is to discover how symbolization occurs through the finding of unconscious fantasy, and to mend the long-standing conceptual split between trauma and fantasy. As is known, severe trauma has the effect of splitting off traumatic memories and shutting down the process of fantasizing and symbolizing. The focus here is on how unconscious fantasy becomes known and articulated in the clinical psychoanalytic setting. More specifically, Ellman and Goodman have asked psychoanalysts with diverse theoretical backgrounds to present cases marked by trauma and somatic pain to demonstrate their ways of discovering and understanding unconscious fantasies in the clinical material.

Ellman and Goodman provide a very brief outline of the major conceptualizations of unconscious fantasy by Freud, Melanie Klein, and Jacob Arlow. (Kleinians use the British spelling phantasy rather than fantasy. I have tried to follow the usage of each contributor.) Freud introduced the idea in his understanding of hysterical symptoms as expressing repressed scenes of seduction involving forbidden wishes. Later he introduced the idea of inborn fantasies such as the primal scene. Klein and Susan Isaacs posit that all mental processes and structures are composed of unconscious phantasies. For Isaacs phantasy is the mental corollary of Freud's instincts. Arlow describes a continuing stream of fantasies throughout life in terms of individual or collective myths.

JAPA Prize Announcements

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association

JAPA Prize

The $1000 JAPA Prize is awarded to the author of the best paper published in the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association during the previous year. All publications, including review articles, research articles, essays and book essays, may be considered for the award. Candidates for the award are nominated by the members of the JAPA Editorial Board, with the final decision being made by the Editor-in-Chief.

JAPA New Author Prize

JAPA's $500 New Author Prize recognizes significant contributions to the journal by new authors. It is awarded for the best published manuscript by a new author, and is presented at the Winter Meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

A new author is considered to be someone who has not previously published in JAPA or any other major psychoanalytic journal and whose psychoanalytic career is in its early stages. A mentoring/editorial group has been established to review submissions by new authors and assist in developing manuscripts for publication in JAPA.

Manuscripts must be submitted via the JAPA submission process, using the online system at http://mc.manuscriptcentral.com/japa. At the time of submission, authors should indicate in the cover letter that the paper is to be considered for the New Author Prize, and may ask that it be directed to the mentoring/editorial group for assistance in manuscript development. Final decision about publication remains with the Editorial Board and the Editor-in-Chief, as does selection of the annual prize-winning paper.

This collection of papers on the interpersonal perspective in psychoanalysis, published between 1960 and 1990, requires considerable historical

361

context if their importance and the editors' purpose in reprinting them are to be understood. By the middle of the twentieth century, psychoanalysis in the United States had achieved a dominant position regarding both the diagnosis and the treatment of mental disorders. Whether it was called classical, traditional, or orthodox, a Freudian version of what constituted “true psychoanalysis” held sway in the medically dominated institutes of the American Psychoanalytic Association (APsaA). To question, no less challenge, the basic assumptions of the Freudian elevation of the infantile unconscious was to risk being told that what you were doing wasn't psychoanalysis. Further, your divergence from the reigning orthodoxy meant you were likely to be told that you weren't a psychoanalyst at all. It is in this context that a number of individuals in Washington, D.C., and New York dissented from the dominant psychoanalytic culture, ultimately developing a series of ideas about theory and technique that in the aggregate came to be known as interpersonal psychoanalysis. These analysts either resigned from APsaA or were forced out, and eventually coalesced around the William Alanson White Institute in New York. They were neither the first dissidents nor the most threatening to American ego psychology at that time; that honor belonged to Melanie Klein and her many followers in England and South America. However, because they were relegated to a separate intellectual sphere, their contributions developed in a separate universe; their papers went unread by traditional psychoanalysts. Interpersonal psychoanalysts might have been well known in their own segregated world, but they too went unrecognized by classical analysts. This separation was fueled on the one hand by the passionate rejection of interpersonal perspectives by traditionalalists and, on the other, by the reaction of interpersonalists who felt unfairly denigrated by the dismissive rejection of their ideas.

The years since 1990 have seen a gradual return of the exiled dissidents of the White Institute. This was aided both by a softening of dogmatic positions taken by traditional analysts and by renewed interest in the “relational turn,” by the then preferred designation for the interpersonal perspective. Psychoanalytic Dialogues, a new journal dedicated to relational thinking, became increasingly read by contemporary traditional analysts. Finally, just last year, the White Institute was invited to join APsaA, after years of frustrating negotiations.

All publications, including review articles, research articles, essays and book essays, may be considered for the award. Candidates for the award are nominated by the members of the JAPA Editorial Board, with the final decision being made by the Editor-in-Chief.

JAPA New Author Prize

JAPA's $500 New Author Prize recognizes significant contributions to the journal by new authors. It is awarded for the best published manuscript by a new author, and is presented at the Winter Meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

A new author is considered to be someone who has not previously published in JAPA or any other major psychoanalytic journal and whose psychoanalytic career is in its early stages. A mentoring/ editorial group has been established to review submissions by new authors and assist in developing manuscripts for publication in JAPA.

Manuscripts must be submitted via the JAPA submission process, using the online system at http://mc.manuscriptcentral.com/ japa. At the time of submission, authors should indicate in the cover letter that the paper is to be considered for the New Author Prize, and may ask that it be directed to the mentoring/editorial group for assistance in manuscript development. Final decision about publication remains with the Editorial Board and the Editor-in-Chief, as does selection of the annual prize-winning paper.

[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Engagement and Reconstruction in a Dream: Commentary on Fischer

Phillip Blumberg

Phillip Blumberg

135 Central Park West, Sulte 1N
New York, NY 10023-2401
E-mall: pb6338@gmail.com

Phillip Blumberg  

In a posthumously published essay, Richard Gottlieb (2017) suggests that much of Freud's reconstruction of the Wolf Man's infantile history reflects specific transference-countertransference engagements occurring during treatment. The reconstructed past is shown to reveal much about warded-off aspects of the analytic present. I will use Gottlieb's ideas in examining the reconstruction presented in Newell Fischer's “A Note on the Indelible.” I offer these thoughts not as a better, more “exact” interpretation of M.H.'s dream but instead as an example of the clinical richness that can emerge when Gottlieb's work is taken into account. In my speculations about the relationship between particular forms of clinical engagement and reconstruction, I hope that some incongruities in the dream narrative and clinical processes provided in “A Note on the Indelible” will yield intriguing questions that can allow a deeper appreciation of both this dream and the treatment.

M.H., an eighty-year-old retired mental health professional, is described as a “handsome gray-haired gentleman … [who] was articulate, thoughtful, and psychologically sophisticated” (p. 94). We are told he was happily married, in good health, financially comfortable, on good terms with his wife, children, and grandchildren, and enjoying retirement by fulfilling his lifelong wish to travel and explore new places. Although several of his close relatives had died in their early seventies, his only stated concern about his health was that he might become “immobilized by various arthritic ailments” (p. 95). M.H. had undergone psychoanalysis several decades earlier and found it “helpful and rewarding” (p. 94).

Creative Listening and the Psychoanalytic Process is a carefully researched, clinically valuable, and deeply personal book “written as a guide for clinicians [who wish] to translate the richness of literary fiction into something clinically useful” (p. 5). Its author, Fred Griffin, argues that Freud's concepts of “psychoanalytic listening and process … focused [primarily] on revelations of the analysand's intrapsychic contents” (p. 3) do not capture the imaginative and active listening required of the modern analyst fully engaged within the transference-countertransference (the analytic space) of an interpersonal/intersubjective clinical psychoanalysis. In addition to carefully exploring the analysand's intrapsychic life, such an analysis allows interpersonal/intersubjective experiences to emerge and be explored within the analytic space. Using Virginia Woolf's To the Lighthouse (1927), Griffin presents a sophisticated primer detailing the elements of creative analytic listening necessary to conduct such analytic work.

Life and Times

Freud in His Time and Ours. By Élisabeth Roudinesco; translated by Catherine Porter. Cambridge: Harvard University Press, 2016, xvi + 580 pp., $35.00 hardcover.

Harry Trosman

Harry Trosman

5841 South Maryland Avenue
Chicago, IL 60637
E-mail: htrosman@yoda.bsd.uchicago.edu

Harry Trosman   Training and Supervising Analyst, Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis; Professor of Psychiatry and Behavioral Neuroscience, University of Chicago.

A biographer of Sigmund Freud is likely to leave a discernible personal imprint on a work. Since we live in a Freudian age today, one is either a Freudian or an anti-Freudian.1 One who is neither is hardly a denizen of the modern world. Élisabeth Roudinesco, a prolific historian and psychoanalyst, has written extensively on psychoanalysis and Jacques Lacan. The present biography of Freud was written in French, published in 2014, and published in in English two years later. It is an account of Freud that emphasizes his social and political position rather than highlighting him as a scientist, a designation he preferred. “Freud always thought that what he was discovering in the unconscious foreshadowed what was happening to people in reality,” she writes. “I have chosen to reverse this proposition and show that what Freud thought he was discovering was at bottom nothing but the product of a society, a familial environment, and a political situation whose signification he interpreted masterfully so as to ascribe it to the work of the unconscious” (p. 4). In this interesting reversal, the emphasis is displaced from seeing psychoanalysis as concerned primarily with psychology and intrapsychic phenomena to viewing them as secondary. Paying particular attention to the era in which Freud lived, the biographer emphasizes a contemporaneous view of his life and work.

The book follows the chronology of its subject's life, starting with his early development, important familial relationships, the “invention” of psychoanalysis, his success, his progress through the First World War to his death. Roudinesco was aided by her access to the Freud Archives, now available in the Library of Congress and open for public inspection.

1The author appreciates the editorial assistance of Sheila Butler.339

The intention of Bárbara Bettocchi and Raúl Fatule, the editors of A Binocular Vision: Psychoanalysis and Philosophy, is to articulate points of connection between these two disciplines, both of which, while

386

maintaining a narrow and often problematic relationship, study the nature of human consciousness and unconsciousness, fantasy and reality. While this would seem an ambitious goal for anything less than a work of several volumes, the editors might have pulled off something even better in this slim volume: an unpretentious and concise series of chapters, two or three to a section, offering glimpses into how certain psychoanalytic constructions are viewed through the binocular vision of deeply curious and philosophically minded analysts.

The contributors are mainly psychoanalysts, including one candidate with some specialization in philosophical studies, and three professors of philosophy. All are Latin Americans and members of psychoanalytic societies or university faculties in Peru, Argentina, or Europe.

The book's title evokes Bion and his use of the term binocular vision, used as an analogy with psychoanalytic vision: “The model is formed by the exercise of a capacity similar to that which is in evidence when the two eyes operate in binocular vision to correlate two views of the same object” (1962, p. 86). One image is created from the fusing of two unique and independent points of view. In this volume, Bettocchi and Fatule organize their contributors' binocular vision around four vertices: the relationship between intrapsychic and intersubjective in regard to recognition and drive; the epistemological status and reach of psychoanalytic concepts; the debate between the rational and the unconscious dimensions; and recent theoretical interdisciplinary proposals.

Francisco Otero begins the first chapter with a close look at the literature on connections between drive, repression, and will, centering the discussion around Schopenhauer's influence on Freud. This influence was quite natural, given the philosopher's prominence in the intellectual atmosphere of the “psychological” nineteenth century. Freud, Otero reminds us, was never able to completely avoid the thinker, even though he openly resisted his ideas, along with Nietzsche's. The resistance had to do with Freud's aversion to metaphysics, accorded not quite the “black sludge” status assigned to occultism in his later warning to Jung, yet regarded early on as sufficiently ungrounded to divert psychoanalysis from the path of scientific positivism. But when it came to deciphering the complexities of being human, sexuality was a rubric for both men, who viewed it as inextricably linked to conflict and the will to live. The latter notion was expanded by Freud to become his concept of eros.

Anne C. Dailey, Evangeline Starr Professor of Law at University of Connecticutt Law School, where she has taught since 1990, is no stranger to psychoanalysis. She has been Erikson Scholar-in-Residence at the Austen Riggs Center and is currently Academic Associate at the Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis. In 2002 she received the CORST prize from the American Psychoanalytic Association for the best interdisciplinary essay; her paper, “Developmental Perspectives on the Ideal of Reason in American Constitutional Law” (Dailey 2007a), was subsequently published in the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association. In 2007 she edited an issue of American Imago (see Dailey 2007b) devoted to the convergence of psychoanalysis and law. Her new book, Law and the Unconscious: A Psychoanalytic Perspective, received the American Psychoanalytic Association's 2018 Courage to Dream Book Prize, an honor it richly deserves.

Law and the Unconscious shows how psychoanalysis grounded in clinical work can infuse the law with the reality of psychological experience. Dailey's book demonstrates the best of interdisciplinary scholarship, where two disparate professions, in this case, law and psychoanalysis, each with a long history of separate traditions and assumptions, can enrich and be enriched by the other, without one discipline colonizing the other. To change metaphors, the best interdisciplinary scholarship can result in a fruitful cross-pollination.

Decades ago, there was a keen interest in using psychoanalytic insights in legal scholarship and practice, but psychoanalysis fell out of favor, and now seldom enters law school discussions or courtrooms.

The womb, a symbol of fertility connoting the female body, nourishes the future baby through holding and the flow of blood. This book takes us into the world of painful perpetrations against women, their bodies, and their psyches, terroristic acts that disrupt their independence and the assertion of their creativity. The papers collected here provide a counterforce, opening freedom and generativity through psychoanalytic understanding, personal experience, and social history. The four editors are psychoanalytically oriented and committed to understanding issues of gender and recognition of women within the historical context of a patriarchal society. All have been active participants in Section III (Women, Gender, and Psychoanalysis) of Division 39 (Division of Psychoanalysis)

A Note on the Indelible

Newell Fischer

Newell Fischer

658 Black Rock Road
Bryn Mawr, PA 19010-1802
E-mail: newell.fischer@gmail.com

Newell Fischer  

psychoanalysis, neonatal trauma, preverbal memory, memorialize, indelible

An eighty-year-old man reports a recurrent nightmare involving a gummy substance being stuffed into his mouth and, despite his anxiety-driven efforts, being unable to remove it. A narrative of his neonatal years is developed and it is speculated that this nightmare is a rekindling of his very distressful breast feeding experience—an experience memorialized in his earliest pre-representational memory. Rich clinical reports of preverbal infant trauma help elucidate the earliest infant-mother dyad.

Leon Hoffman

Leon Hoffman

167 East 67th Street
New York, NY 10065
E-mail: hoffman.leon@gmail.com

Leon Hoffman  

Richard Gottlieb was a clinician, scholar, teacher, and close personal friend. Since academic and intellectual discourse was such a central part of Richard's life and of his relationships with colleagues and friends, it is fitting that we remember him with this panel and these papers that build on his final academic contribution, “Reconstruction in a Two-Person World May Be More about the Present Than the Past: Freud and the Wolf Man, an Illustration” (Gottlieb 2017).1

In that paper he notes that the psychoanalytic process of reconstruction has not been reexamined from the perspective of today's two-person formulations. He notes too that previous observers have implicated the analyst, and his emotional involvement, as influences that may distort the valid recovery of memories. While others have written that the transference and the reconstructed past are interdependent, Gottlieb stresses the important influence of the present on memories of the past.

The contribution of this paper is that the recovery of some presumed past event in the patient's life may reflect a version of the current analytic relationship. In other words, the reconstruction may say more about the analytic present than about the patient's historical past.

1This APsaA panel was convened February 18, 2018, at the New York Hilton. Originally proposed by Richard, it was held in his memory little more than a year after his death on January 31, 2017. Among his other accomplishments, Richard was for many years an Associate Editor of JAPA.

[[RunningHead]]

“The Fullness of the Infinite”: Introduction to Disillusionment Papers

Adele Tutter

Adele Tutter

300 Central Park West
New York, NY 10024-1513
E-mail: a.tutter@mac.com

Adele Tutter  

In his 1896 short story “Disillusionment” (Enttäuschung: disillusionment, disappointment, disenchantment), Thomas Mann creatively interrogates the developmental process of disillusionment. Only a few pages long, the work consists of a story-within-a-story: the narrator, a young tourist in Venice not yet disenchanted with life (the exotic city fails to disappoint) tells of a curious encounter with a stranger of indeterminate age who volunteers the story of his life. The man explains that since his youth he has suffered from the malady of “great and general disappointment with which everything, all of life, has in store.” His life began in “a clergyman's family, in quite a small town.” There, he says,

We breathed a strange atmosphere, compact of pulpit rhetoric, of large words for good and evil, beautiful and base, which I bitterly hate, since perhaps they are to blame for all my sufferings. For me life consisted utterly of these large words. … From man I expected divine virtue or hair-raising wickedness, from life either ravishing loveliness or else consummate horror; and I was full of avidity for that and of a profound, tormented yearning for a larger reality, for experience of not matter what kind [Mann 1896, p. 738].

Life would vex the Venetian gentleman, never fulfilling his “magnificent expectation of life”—a treasured illusion emblematized by his beloved “large words.” Nevertheless, he struggled to maintain the longings that he “zealously fed” with “a thousand books and the works of all the poets. Ah, how I have learned to hate them,” he confides, “those poets who chalked up their large words on all the wall of life. … I came to think of every large word as a lie or mockery” (p. 739).
