An Analytic Journey: From the Art of Archery to the Art of Psychoanalysis. By Marilla Aisenstein. London: Karnac Books, 2017, xviii + 231 pp., $36.95 paperback.

Arlene Richards

Arlene Richards

200 East 89th Street
New York, NY 10128-4308
E-mail: arlenerichards89@gmail.com

Arlene Richards   Training and Supervising Analyst, Contemporary Freudian Society and the International Psychoanalytical Association; Fellow, Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research, New York.

A wry joke that spread among North American psychoanalysts went something like this: “How do you know a mafioso is a Lacanian? He makes you

590

a proposition you can't understand.” But the inability to understand was partly a refusal to understand. Now that Lacan has had a great influence on French psychoanalysts, and through them, on some Latin American and British analysts, as well as some in China and other places, it may be time to figure out what so many analysts find useful in his ideas.

This book by Marilla Aisenstein makes this more possible now than it has been in the decades since Lacan first conceived and taught his then radical and apparently “overly obscure” theories. Aisenstein makes no attempt to explicate Lacan; rather, she holds up a mirror to Lacanian ideas as they have influenced others. I want to try to understand her ideas from the point of view of contemporary conflict theory.

Aisenstein gives us a picture of the evolution of her thought and that of the Paris Psychosomatic School, where she was trained and eventually became president. Speaking for that society, she differentiates French thinking about technique, as centering on “a surprise effect which short-circuits secondary processes, aiming directly at primary processes” (p. 6). But she also asserts that for the Psychosomatic School, affect, not language, is the conceptual tool of analysis. The Lacanians, by contrast, privilege language. In addition, she differentiates the schools by emphasizing the psychosomatic group's attention to countertransference. According to Aisenstein, Lacanians do not regard countertransference as crucial to understanding patients. This concise account of the differences between Lacanian clinical theory and that of the Psychosomatic School seems to me to be quite useful.

In another key, Aisenstein cites the use of countertransference in understanding analysands’ communications. Again, this seems now to be a universal tenet embraced by all schools of psychoanalysis other than the Lacanian. The others differ only on whether interpretations must always include explicit reference to transference-countertransference. Modern conflict theory allows for both transference interpretations and interpretations that do not make transference explicit. So too does French psychosomatic theory.

Psychoanalysis and the Holocaust

Psychoanalysis and Holocaust Testimony. Edited by Dori Laub and Andreas Hamburger. New York: Routledge, 2017, xvi + 324 pp., $190.00 hardcover, $52.95 paperback.

Ilany Kogan

Ilany Kogan

2, Mohaliver Street
Rehovat
ISRAEL
E-mail: Ilanyk@yahoo.com

Ilany Kogan   Training Analyst, Israel Psychoanalytic Society.

This rich book is an inquiry into the sequelae of extreme social traumatization. It focuses on the trauma of the Holocaust and its impact on the psyches of survivors, as well as on the emotional atmosphere that surrounded them in Israeli society in the post-Holocaust era, where it took the form of a conspiracy of silence and denial.

The book contains four sections: Part I addresses the basic concept of social trauma from a psychoanalytic perspective. Werner Bohleber examines the difficulties psychoanalysis initially faced in conceptualizing traumatic phenomena. He stresses the importance in therapy of acknowledging the reality of traumatic events, a fact which for many years was completely ignored or denied by psychoanalysts. Dori Laub and Nanette Auerhahn, in the seminal “Knowing and Not Knowing: Forms of Traumatic Memory,” describe a continuum of forms of knowing psychic trauma, each form progressively representing a consciously deeper “level of knowing,” and hence increasing integration. In chapter 3, Laub postulates the absence of an internal dialogic “thou” as causing a shutdown of the narrative and the symbolizing process. In chapter 4, Andreas Hamburger distinguishes between individual and social (especially genocidal) trauma. Social trauma affects the individual's supportive environment and involves the surrounding society in the traumatic process, thus having an impact on the resulting post-traumatic disorder. In the next chapter, Francoise Davoine and Jean-Max Gaudillière discuss clinical cases. The last chapter of Part I, by Suzanne Kaplan and Andreas Hamburger, explores the specificity of traumatization in cases of children and adolescents, well illustrated with findings from Kaplan's videotaped testimony work with child survivors of the Rwandan genocide.

The Freud-Bleuler Correspondence: French Edition

Sigmund Freud, Eugen Bleuler: Lettres, 1904-1937. Edited by Michael Schröter. Translated by Dorian Astor. Paris: Éditions Gallimard, 2016, 320 pp., €32.00.

Hélène Tessier

Hélène Tessier

219 Laurier Street, West
Montreal, Quebec
H2T 2N9
CANADA
E-mail: tessierhelene7@gmail.com

Hélène Tessier   Member, Canadian Psychoanalytic Society (Société psychanalytique de Montreal); Professor, Faculty of Human Sciences and Philosophy, Saint Paul University, Ottawa; Vice President, Scientific Council of the Fondation Jean Laplanche / Nouveaux Fondements pour la Psychanalyse.

The correspondence between Sigmund Freud and Eugen Bleuler was published in Basel in 2012 under the title “Ich bin zuversichtlich, wir erobern bald die Psychiatrie”: Briefwechsel 1904-1937. The main German title—in English, “I am confident we soon will conquer psychiatry”—is a quotation from Freud's letter of December 30, 1906, to Bleuler. The sentiment underlines an important theme of Freud's interest in Bleuler's work: his hope that Bleuler's sympathy for psychoanalysis would secure the new field's position within academic psychiatry. The French edition of the letters, published in Paris in 2016, takes a slightly different tack. It dispenses with Freud's quote in the title, which simply reads: Lettres, 1904-1937. Nevertheless, it proposes a reading orientation that like the German edition highlights the relations between psychoanalysis and psychiatry: both the series introduction by Michel Gribinski and the introduction by Thomas Lepoutre and François Villa, “Freud et la psychiatrie,” insist on the relevance of the Freud-Bleuler correspondence in understanding the contemporary attitude of psychiatry toward psychoanalysis.

Since Christian Maetzener's review of the German edition gives a thorough account of the context of the publication and of the correspondence, I will briefly comment on the debates between Freud and Bleuler over theoretical issues and then present a few reflections on contemporary psychoanalysis inspired by Lepoutre and Villa's paper, as well as by the correspondence itself.

Richard Zeitner

Richard Zeitner

1007 SW Walnut Street
Blue Springs, MO 64015
E-mail: richzeitner@gmail.com

Richard Zeitner   Training and Supervising Analyst, Greater Kansas City Psychoanalytic Institute; Assistant Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University of Missouri Kansas City School of Medicine; Adjunct Instructor, International Psychotherapy Institute; North American Co-chair, Committee on Family and Couple Psychoanalysis, International Psychoanalytical Association.

David Scharff and Elizabeth Palacios's edited volume is the outcome of a most creative endeavor—couple and family psychoanalysts from around the world assembing to explore a range of new ideas on a project still in its early stages—the application of psychoanalytic concepts to the study and treatment of the couple dyad and the family constellation.

What is perhaps the greatest virtue of this book is its international scope. While so much of the literature we read as analysts is ensconced in our immediate culture, Scharff and Palacios have placed cultural differences squarely at the center of this collection. Although we know that these differences are important considerations in clinical work, perhaps nowhere are they more important than in work with families and couples. Cultures vary in how the family is structured and shaped over time, including its values, expectations, what is normative and what is not, how the generations relate, what are acceptable, even expected influences by one generation on another, versus what is exceptional.

Julie Nagel

444 South Main Street, Suite 203

Julie Nagel  

This paper discusses stage fright as seen primarily in classical music performers. A confluence of narcissistic sensitivities, developmental issues, shame dynamics, physical injury and pain, and terrors pertaining to both psychic and bodily disintegration can fuel anxiety reactions in many people but hold particular relevance for the performing musician. Two classical music performers who struggled with stage fright are discussed: one was in treatment with the author; the other is the author herself, reflecting on her own performance anxiety as revealed through her analysis and her countertransference to her patient.

Paola Solano and Luca Quagelli

Luca Quagelli

Via Mendozza 37
16166 Genova ITALY
E-mail: paola.solano@alice.it

22 Avenue Gallieni
92400 Courbevoie FRANCE

Luca Quagelli  

transitional space, transitional phenomena, symbolization, psychosis, schizophrenia, psychotic indifferentiation, countertransference, transference, concreteness, therapeutic mediations

Clinical material from the treatment of a highly destructive schizophrenic patient is used to demonstrate the role and function of therapeutic mediations in promoting transformation and symbolization. Use of the Squiggle Game as a therapeutic mediation is shown to sustain the therapeutic process and to facilitate working through of the obscure and complex dynamics commonly seen in the treatment of psychotic patients. The Squiggle Game presents a first transitional space entailing both the concreteness of psychosis and the potential for symbolization provided by psychoanalysis. The game becomes the first meeting ground for the progressive encounters of the therapeutic couple, primarily because in it the violent destructiveness of psychosis is partly deflected in a way that fosters development of the transference relationship. Step-by-step emotional transformations gained through the Squiggle Game are reported and discussed, together with the patient's need to rely on nonverbal communicative modes to bring early traumatic experiences that never reached verbalization into treatment. This working through process furthered development of the dyad's intense transference-countertransference dynamics, which stimulated construction of a link between here-and-now and there-and-then in sessions, leading to the patient's integration and a sense of the life-historical significance of her experience.

Winnicott's Antigone

The Piggle: An Account of the Psychoanalytic Treatment of a Little Girl. By D. W. Winnicott. Madison, CT: International Universities Press, 1977, 201 pp.

T. Downey

T. Downey

132 Sachems Head Road
Guilford, CT 06437
E-mail: twdowney@comcast.net

T. Downey   Clinical Professor, Yale Child Study Center, Yale School of Medicine; Training and Supervising Analyst, Adult, Adolescent, and Child Psychoanalysis, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis.

In 1964 Donald Woods Winnicott, a sixty-eight-year-old, once scintillating psychoanalyst in the twilight of his life, afflicted with severe heart disease and angina, his career fading toward darkness, enters into a therapeutic encounter with a two-year-old girl nicknamed “the Piggle,” who night and day struggles with gusts of psychotic rage and depression following the birth of a hated sister. It is my premise that in the treatment that follows it is not just that Winnicott heals the girl according to the traditional treatment paradigm; it is that in the process they act as change agents for each other. In an imperfect but effective therapeutic endeavor, old man and young child join together to overcome their mutual preoccupation with death and destruction and to set things right in a way that allows the Piggle to rejoin life stronger than ever and permits Winnicott to start to reconcile his remarkably lively life with his impending death. “Piggle” means “pickle.” Ironically, both parties here are in one, and each helps the other out of it.

Through many readings over many years, I have been struck by how much this story resonates with two plays in Sophocles’ Oedipus trilogy. In Oedipus at Colonus, Antigone guides her father, Oedipus, blind and approaching death, to a sacred grove where with her assistance he sheds his remorse and dies a death sanctioned by the gods. Both father and daughter are changed in the process. In Antigone's case her maturation in courage, altruism, and morality becomes the subject of the final play, Antigone. In parallel fashion to the Sophoclean plays, I am suggesting, the Piggle became Winnicott's Antigone, instancing a universal feature of therapeutic engagements: change occurs differentially in them, but each party is changed.

Bibliophilia and Transitional Phenomena

When I was seventeen I took Sigmund Freud for my first psychoanalytic lover. Who knew? There were no intellectual bones in my family's body.

Spatial Translation and Regression in Dreams: The Nicholas Young Phenomenon

Leon Balter

Leon Balter

544 East 86th Street, 1SW
New York, NY 10028
E-mail: lbaltermd@aol.com

Leon Balter  

dream series, regression, threshold symbolism, Scherner's Law, Herbert Silberer

The Nicholas Young phenomenon involves an immediate shift in dreams to more regressive mental functioning whenever, in the manifest ideational content, the dreamer or protagonist crosses a topographical boundary or threshold (spatial translation). This phenomenon thus furthers the regression typical in dreams. Eight examples of the Young phenomenon are presented. An attempt to explain this highly specialized phenomenon addresses dreams in series, or dream sections in series, and refers to Scherner's Law, which covers their sequential trend toward regression. Referring to the mind's self-observing capacity, Silberer's “symbolism of the threshold” is invoked to explain the spatial translation of the Young phenomenon.

[[RunningHead]]

How Do You Get There from Here? Introduction to Fischer

Anne Erreich

Anne Erreich

E-mail: anne@erreich.com

Anne Erreich  

Psychoanalysis has been bashed for many reasons, but a particularly prominent complaint refers to its postdictive methodology. That is, we are loath to make future predictions for an individual; rather, we aim to provide a post hoc account of what most likely happened in the past or, more accurately, the reconstruction of an individual's experience of the past. The criticism is that a methodology that can readily account for any outcome whatever is too pliable. If it accounts for everything, it ultimately accounts for nothing.

In recent decades the traditional view of transference and the value of reconstruction has been challenged by psychoanalysts themselves. The challenge includes both the reinterpretation of transference as a here-and-now phenomenon rather than a there-and-then construction, a view championed by Kleinians and others, and the notion that there is no there-and-then to reconstruct via transference phenomena, that transference is co-created de novo in each analytic dyad, an account held by many relational theorists. On both views, the role of reconstruction has been challenged, minimized, or even eliminated.

Richard Gottlieb's posthumously published paper (2017) attempts to demonstrate that Freud's reconstruction of the Wolf Man's infantile history reflects the transference-countertransference entanglement in their ongoing analytic work. In this respect, his essay challenges the false dichotomy between the there-and-then (genetic reconstruction) and the here-and-now transference-countertransference fantasy enactments in the analytic dyad. Given the ubiquity of transference, it is inevitable that the provenance of here-and-now transference fantasies and enactments will be found in historical, there-and-then traumatic events. It is a technical decision to choose to interpret the former over the latter, at least in the opening phases of an analysis, as a corrective to intellectualization's being favored over one's in vivo experience in the transference.

Discussion: A Crisis of Disillusionment

Lynne Zeavin

Lynne Zeavin

80 University Place, 5th floor
New York, NY 10003-4564
E-mail: drlynne.zeavin@gmail.com

Lynne Zeavin  

The papers in this section offer us a variety of ways to think about disillusionment and the illusions that necessarily precede and give rise to it. My hope is that the reader will consider each paper interdependently with the others. Rather than offering the individual discussion each paper warrants, my comments will treat these four papers as something of a cluster. I will highlight some of the papers' salient contributions, and conclude by discussing the presence of illusionment and disillusionment in the contemporary American scene.

Placing illusion at the center of creative thought and psychic health, Stephen Seligman focuses on its foundational, generative aspects. He emphasizes the role of illusion in allowing us to believe that what we create really exists. “Adequate infant care,” he writes, “protects the baby from being anxious about her needs being met or the possibility that her environment might let her down. … the parents' care allows the baby to be carefree” (p. 269). Debunking illusion can be dangerous, he says. He argues for the primary place of illusion, not as a regression moving us away from reality, but as a vital psychic capacity. He treats illusion as something to perpetuate, something to sustain throughout the life cycle.

Our other authors see it differently.

Jane Tillman's emphases darken the picture of illusion. She argues that, regardless of the necessity of illusion, one's actual capacity to live in reality demands the ability to sustain disillusion. For Tillman, illusion functions to cover over the incurable wound at the heart of human experience, a wound that is always there and must be faced. Contextualized within a review of psychoanalytic developmental theories of disillusion, Tillman's paper offers a series of vignettes from a research study of participants who have survived a near lethal suicide attempt.

Faculty. New York Psychoanalytic Institute.

Howard Foster   Training and Supervising Analyst, Pittsburgh Psychoanalytic Center; Clinical Assistant Professor, Department of Psychiatry, University of Pittsburgh School of Medicine.

Bruce Fink began as an academic psychologist and is today a Lacanian analyst and noted translator of Lacan. His graduate and undergraduate courses on Freud provide some background for this book, indicating as they do that although he is a Lacanian he has wide knowledge and a serious interest when it comes to Freud. Fink's interest in clinical technique that directly impacts the unconscious is demonstrated in his 2010 paper, “Against Understanding: Why Understanding Should Not Be Viewed as an Essential Aim of Psychoanalytic Treatment”: “psychoanalysis clearly aims at the establishment of a new relationship between the ego and the unconscious whereby the ego no longer rejects and represses so many things” (p. 263). In addition, displaying his cultural sophistication and playfulness, Fink writes mystery novels in which the detective is based loosely on Lacan. He brings all these talents together in this introduction to the clinical Freud.

Fink's goals for the book are broader than understanding Freud clinically. The title and the picture on the dust jacket give clues. The book's subtitle is Techniques for Everyday Practice, but the picture is not of Freud's couch and office. The picture, rather, shows the pleasing décor of a contemporary analyst's office, yet with familiar Freudian images: the couch, a chair, a table with an open book. The title and the picture seem to underscore Fink's wish to place us in Freud's shoes in his journey to discover free association and dream analysis to help patients explore their unconscious. He extends Freud's techniques to explore the unconscious by showing how he and Lacan grasp and impact the unconscious so that patients will live more satisfying and meaningful lives. He wants clinicians to have more tools in their toolboxes. As Warren Poland, in “Reading Fiction and the Psychoanalytic Experience: Proust on Reading and on Reading Proust” (2003), points out, a book can change the reader. Fink challenges the reader to change like Freud did: by discovering knowledge, skills, and attitudes that will help patients.

Andrea Celenza's Erotic Revelations: Clinical Applications and Perverse Scenarios is a well-crafted collection of her papers. Some are new works, some are edited versions of old ones, and all are important contributions to the continuing revision of psychoanalytic models of gender and sexual development, efforts that have now traversed the fertile, variegated, and somewhat unruly landscape of the late-twentieth and early-twenty-first century.

Following a lucid introduction—“Transcending Boundaries”—are two main sections in which she addresses theoretical and clinical aspects of the manner in which erotic desires and unconscious fantasies express essential ways of being and shape how analyst and analysand engage the analytic situation. The first part, “Erotics Embodied: Transferences and Countertransferences,” draws mainly from previously published work.

Most fundamentally, she assumes that we enter the world embodied, characterized by sensate receptivity to feeling and affective aliveness. Echoing Laplanche (1997), Celenza asserts that the mother, immediately and universally, “transmits her enigmatic sexual unconscious to the infant” (p. 1) and that in this way one's gender and sexuality are present and emergent from the very first.

The Digital Age on the Couch: Psychoanalytic Practice and New Media is a stunning contribution on the meaning and effect of technology vis-à-vis contemporary society, our lives, and the therapies we conduct. Happily, this book is the work of a single author, developing her views beyond those expressed in her co-edited book, the timely Psychoanalysis in the Technoculture Era (Lemma and Caparrotta 2014). Part I, “Outside In,” comprises three chapters on the real and virtual body, sexual identity formation, and disintermediation of desire (featuring a cogent analysis of the movie Her). Part II, “Inside Out,” has a chapter on technology-mediated psychotherapy, concluding with five pointers on how to conduct mediated therapy responsibly. Its other chapter focuses on the “digital transference” of patients who use technology to invade their analyst's privacy and preference for neutrality, express hostility and longing, and defend against rejection.

Finding Freedom: Exploring the Relationship between Agency, Motility, and Aggression

Tom Wooldridge

Tom Wooldridge

536 Mission Street
San Francisco, CA 94105
E-mail: twooldridge@ggu.edu

Tom Wooldridge  

agency, freedom, determinism, psychic determinism, compatibilism, motility, aggression

After an overview of the opposed concepts of freedom and psychic determinism, and of the compatibilist reconciliation between the two that is most appropriate for psychoanalysis, the importance of agency as a psychoanalytic idea is considered. In particular, two ways in which the term agency may be used are examined: agency as fact and agency as experience. The former refers to the degree of relative freedom an individual possesses from “inner” constraints, whereas the latter refers to the experience of oneself as having an active impact on the self, other people, and/or the world. Importantly, these two senses are not coextensive. With this distinction in place, it is argued that agency as experience is rooted in the infant's earliest experiences of motility. As an extension of Winnicott's reflections (1950) on the relation between motility and aggression, it is argued that inasmuch as it involves the overcoming of opposition, aggression is integral to the developmental unfolding of the experience of agency. Further developmental conditions needed for a full experience of agency are noted. Ultimately, it is suggested, agency is central to the psychoanalytic project itself.

(How) Can We Write about Our Patients?

Sarah Ackerman

Sarah Ackerman

53 South Main Street, Suite 311
Hanover, NH 03755
E-mail: ackerman@post.harvard.edu

Sarah Ackerman  

ethics, clinical writing, case reports

The ethical underpinnings of writing about patients are explored, the question of how best to undertake the writing of case reports being subordinated to a more general question about the ethics of choosing how or whether to write. An unsolvable paradox is encountered here: that we need to write or speak about our clinical work in order to conceptualize and understand the work we are doing, but that in the very gesture of doing so, we are breaking a fundamental bond with the patient. This conundrum is viewed from a number of vantage points. The controversy about how best to go about writing clinical accounts is first addressed, after which the literature is reviewed to draw out the ethical conflicts that writing about patients engenders in the patient. Next attention is given to undercurrents in the analyst's motivation to write, again drawing on current literature. Finally, a consideration is provided of how, based on what we might learn from this review, these problems can be addressed.

Stanley Coen

Even poor Mademoiselle Dombreuil, by dint of functioning as lightning conductor, was drawn into the turbid and laughing vortex, just as psychiatrists become infected and succumb to the frenzies of their patients.

—Giuseppe di Lampedusa, The Leopard (1958, p. 180)

… the analyst becomes the patient, espouses his delusional system. … good criticism involves a willingness, a desire, to enter into the delusional system of texts, to espouse their hallucinated vision, in an attempt to master and be mastered by their power of conviction.

—Peter Brooks (1987, p. 347)

Othello: Put out the light, and then put out the light. / If I quench thee, thou flaming minister, / I can again thy former light restore. / … I will kill thee / and love thee after. … / . … This sorrow's heavenly, / it strikes where it doth love.

—Shakespeare, Othello (5.2.7-23).

Freud (1915) had good reason to warn us to be careful how we behave with our analysands. That warning led, however, to too much pressure on us to stifle our needs, temptations, and feelings in work with our patients, lest they get out of hand.

Plenary address, American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, February 16, 2018.

Briefer versions of this paper were presented at the Eighth Joint International Psychoanalytic Conference, Reykjavik, August 2016; at Das Berliner Psychoanalytisches Institut (Karl-Abraham-Institut) to CAPS Group 4 and a group of German psychoanalysts, October 2016; and to the panel “Desire and Dread in the Psychoanalytic Situation,” International Psychoanalytical Association Congress, Buenos Aires, July 2017. “Playing Out” Our “Playing in the Dark”: Racial Enactments and Psychoanalytic Institutions

Michelle Stephens

Michelle Stephens

20 West 74th Street
New York, NY 10023
E-mail: mchllstephens@gmail.com

Michelle Stephens  

At this year's University Forum on February 16, 2018, Jonathan Holloway, Claudia Rankine, Jeffrey Prager, and I convened a panel discussion titled “Racism in America, Part II.” The panel was envisioned as a follow-up to last year's discussion of the current status of race and race relations in the United States. The previous panel came on the heels of the 2016 Presidential election and, not surprisingly, the comments of panelists Darryl Pinckney and Larry Bobo looked both inward and outward as they reflected on their personal reactions to the state of race relations in American society. One year later, we assembled again in February and assigned ourselves a slightly different task, one related to our various institutional roles.

Jonathan Holloway, previously a professor of history and Dean of Yale College, had just begun his appointment as Provost of Northwestern University. Claudia Rankine, the Frederick Iseman Professor of Poetry at Yale University and recent winner of the National Book Critics Circle Award for her 2014 poetry collection Citizen: An American Lyric, had just announced her intention to use her MacArthur “genius” grant to fund a Racial Imaginary Institute in Manhattan. Jeffrey Prager, Professor of Sociology at UCLA and former Dean of the New Center for Psychoanalysis in Los Angeles, served as the panel's discussant; and I, as chair of the panel, brought my experiences as Dean of the Humanities at Rutgers University, and as a cofounder of the Study Group on Race and Psychoanalysis at the William Alanson White Institute in New York.

Entering One's Own Life as an Aim of Clinical Psychoanalysis

Riccardo Lombardi

Riccardo Lombardi

Via dei Fienaroli 36
Roma 00153
ITALY
E-mail: dr.riccardolombardi54@gmail.com

Riccardo Lombardi  

body-mind dissociation, devitalization, life-death confusion, working through, transference to the body

The historical development of psychoanalysis has demonstrated that the aim of clinical work can change as the patient population changes. One of the main tasks of psychoanalytic working through today is to help difficult patients trapped in imitative dynamics and “never-to-be-born selves” enter a life of their own. Particular emphasis is given to activating a body-mind relationship, catalyzing emergence from the unrepressed unconscious, and constructing space-time parameters in relation to the most primitive and undifferentiated emotional experiences. Two clinical cases are presented, in the first of which the analyst found himself invested with an intense devitalization that tested his capacity to be present. In the second case the analyst was confronted by the necessity of stimulating the birth of basic functions of mental notation in relation to blind and dangerous acting out. The confrontation in the analytic relationship mobilized the patient's internal resources of self-observation and self-containment, from which the capacity to exist and be present to the self could emerge.

Illusion as a Basic Psychic Principle: Winnicott, Freud, Oedipus, and Trump

Stephen Seligman

Stephen Seligman

3667 Sacramento Street
San Francisco, CA 94118
E-mail: stephen.seligman@ucsf.edu

Stephen Seligman  

illusion, Winnicott, play, Oedipus, mourning, Trump, disillusionment, politics, Freud, omnipotence, death instinct

Illusion can be viewed as a creative engagement with the world, and as a central psychic motivation and capacity, rather than as a form of self-deception. Winnicott and other Middle Group writers have understood integrative, imaginative illusion as an essential part of healthy living and psychosocial development. As such, it emerges and presents itself in a variety of ways, in transaction with the realities that support or degrade it. In its absence, varied difficulties in living ensue. To elaborate and illustrate this conceptualization, Freud's notion that the oedipus complex is resolved is reconsidered as a creative misreading of Sophocles' Oedipus trilogy, one based on the plausible illusion of a civilizing psychosocial development that would serve as a protective bastion against his experience of the political chaos and violence of the first decades of twentieth-century European history. Finally, the place of illusion and disillusionment among those most disillusioned by the recent election of Donald Trump in the United States is considered in relation to the recent right-wing populist turn.

Playing in the Darkness: Use of the Object and Use of the Subject

Steven Cooper

Steven Cooper

875 Massachusetts Avenue, Suite 54
Cambridge, MA 02139
E-mail: stevencooper03@gmail.com

Steven Cooper  

play, symbolization, absence, transference, countertransference, metaphor, enactment, object use, paradox, unconscious fantasy, translation

Play in the context of the patient's sense of absence, loss, and compromised capacities for symbolization can be a link between unsymbolized experience and greater capacities for representation. Winnicott's concepts of play evolved as one of the ways that analysts translate unconscious and unrepresented experience. For many patients who have experienced absence, the analyst and the analytic setting are subjected to the patient's unconscious efforts to destroy and negate meaning and relatedness. For the analyst to be “used” as an object to be destroyed and to survive destruction, he must become a subject in the mind of the patient and in his own mind as analyst within the intersubjective field. The analyst's work with his own resistance is vital to becoming a changing subject and an object available for play in the psychoanalytic process.

Assembling Justice: Reviving Nonhuman Subjectivities to Examine Institutional Betrayal around Sexual Misconduct

Katie Gentile

Katie Gentile

524 West 59th Street
New York, NY 10019
E-mail: kgentile@jjay.cuny.edu

Katie Gentile  

sexual boundary violations, institutional betrayal, restorative justice, bystander intervention, assembled subjectivities, temporality, sexual trauma, witnessing

While some colleges have adopted bystander intervention and restorative justice practices to address sexual misconduct as a community issue, psychoanalytic institutes facing crises of sexual misconduct have typically relied on the tried-and-true tactic of identifying the neoliberal individual as the sole site of trouble. Successful strategies used in colleges can be applied to the psychoanalytic setting by focusing on institutional betrayal. This systemic, community-based approach decenters not only individual bodies but human subjectivities. Here institutions become not just containers for group or networked affect, as if affect itself emanates from individuals into an institution. Agency and affect emerge through networks, not individual bodies, and institutions are active agents in violent betrayal. Acknowledging the ways the nonhuman and the human co-emerge creates the space to hold the vitalities and agencies of both, including multiple potentialities for (re)traumatization, resistance, and transformation.

Upon entering the Freud Museum in London, one is struck by the extensive collection of antiquities (figurines, busts, statues) on display from ancient Egypt, Greece, Rome, and other cultures. Freud was fascinated with ancient civilizations and collected these objects throughout his career; as Harold Blum has noted, the Wolf Man once gave Freud an Egyptian female figure with a miter. Freud believed that bringing pathogenic unconscious mental contents into the light of conscious awareness was a process akin to recovering “buried treasures.” In fact, his office was arranged so that from his chair, positioned at the head of the couch, he could view and contemplate these antiquities during sessions (Lane and Weihs 2010). He believed that unconscious mental contents were a kind of fully formed buried treasure that were kept out of awareness through defensive processes. In many ways, this view of the unconscious lives on today.

Avner Bergstein

Avner Bergstein

14 Nahum Street
Ramat Gan 5223344
ISRAEL
E-mail: avnerbergstein@gmail.com

Avner Bergstein  

alpha function, beta screen, Bion, complexity, psychotic part of the personality, unrepressed unconscious

The psychotic part of the personality is seen as a multidimensional mental realm that is fully comprehensible only through intuition and tolerance of approximations, transience, and the notion of infinity. It is suggested that a major differentiating factor between the psychotic and nonpsychotic parts of the personality is the capacity to tolerate the infinite complexity of the human mind. With the use of mathematical concepts, Bion tries to describe the state of mind required of the analyst who endeavors to tread on psychotic territories of the personality. Bion is in effect trying to describe the intensity, violence, and fortitude of the transformation of the emotional experience generated by the psychotic as opposed to the nonpsychotic part of personality. Viewed from this vertex, transference is the path by which the unrepressed and unrepresentable unconscious can evolve and express itself. It is the function by which the individual can live through for the first time aspects of his mental life that have never been experienced. This is illustrated with a number of clinical vignettes highlighting different aspects of our capacity to get in touch with this nonsensuous realm of the psychoanalytic encounter.

Adele Tutter

Adele Tutter

124 West 79th Street, Suite 1C

Adele Tutter  

sexuality, transference, erotic transference, maternal transference, Adam and Eve, analytic love, Hans Loewald, Harold Searles, Ella Freeman Sharpe, Julia Kristeva, countertransference, Freudian theory

An erotics of knowing is posited that comprises embodied aspects of psychological and emotional closeness, and derives not from transference dynamics but from psychological and emotional intimacy—both component and consequence of the analytic process. The experience of knowing and being known is invested with erotism via its interpenetrative and inter-receptive aspects; regardless of gender, to know the other is to enter a hidden interior “space” that represents that person's embodied inner world. Yet the interrogation of the intrinsic relationship between knowing and loving is stunningly absent from the psychoanalytic literature. This historical neglect is traced to a split in the discourse presaged by Freud's essay on transference love, which distinguishes between the qualified reality of the erotic transference and the de-erotized but “real” construct of the “analytic love” relationship. A more recent split relocates erotism to the maternal transference, divesting it of aggression and oedipal sexuality. These splits constitute a vigorous collective defense against engaging with the erotics of knowing: from Oedipus to Genesis, our forbidden fruit.

Paul Ricoeur found refuge at the University of Chicago in 1971, having fled the student turmoil at the Sorbonne, where he had taught, and at Nanterre, where he was dean.1 At Chicago, he published two major book-length studies associated with psychoanalysis and an essay on establishing psychoanalytic proof (Ricoeur 1970, 1977a,b).

I begin with two vignettes of Ricoeur as a teacher to indicate his character, which was foundational to his thinking and his values. First is a memory I have of a class Ricoeur taught with Stephen Toulmin, a student of Wittgenstein. In that class, Ricoeur could express much with a gesture: his finger scalloping the air in a line, he showed us how various aesthetic genres, despite their diverse, discipline-specific criteria, could nevertheless be connected, if in a subterranean way. For example, architecture might use the proportionate criteria of Vitruvius, or painting the six criteria of Lommazzo; in poetry, Borges's suggestion for aesthetic criteria might be adopted, or in music Leonard Bernstein's six criteria for judging what is good. Each discipline has its aesthetic criteria. Nevertheless, one can sense aesthetic correspondences, a kind of universal synesthesia, across disciplines. Take, for example, Balanchine's choreography for Concerto Barocco: its “look” captures the “sound” of Bach's Double Violin Concerto and its flowing movement. The dancing pair play with each other and contrapuntally against the “continuo” (the corps de ballet) behind them. Ricoeur's scalloped air danced the correspondences.

Second, I recall Ricoeur's trembling reaction to Jacques Derrida, his former student, after the latter's mocking presentation at a conference held at the university in 1979 (Derrida 1980). This episode showed a different, but related, aspect of Ricoeur's character. Derrida was one of several presenters at the conference, the topic of which was narrative as a

Dionne Powell

Dionne Powell

295 Central Park West
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: dp70@cumc.columbia.edu

Dionne Powell  

race, psychoanalysis, diversity, African American, Black Mental Health, psychotherapy, racial bias, cross-cultural treatments, silence, racism

Both historically and currently, assaults on the black body and mind have been ubiquitous in American society, posing a counterargument to America as a postracial, color-blind society. Yet the collective silence of psychoanalysts on this societal reality limits our ability to explore, teach, and treat the effects, both interpersonal and intrapsychic, of race, racism, racialized trauma, and implicit bias and privilege. This silence, which challenges our relevance as a profession, must be explored in the context of America's racialized identity as an outgrowth of slavery and institutional racism. Racial identifications that maintain whiteness as a construct privileged over otherness are an obstacle to conducting analytic work. Examples of work with racial tensions and biases illustrate its therapeutic potential. The challenge for us as clinicians is to acknowledge and explore our racial bias, ignorance, blind spots, and privilege, along with identifications with the oppressed and the oppressor, as contributors to our silence.

Richard Tuch   Former Dean of Training, New Center for Psychoanalysis, Los Angeles; Training and Supervising Analyst, New Center for Psychoanalysis and the Psychoanalytic Center of California; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, David Geffen School of Medicine at UCLA.

The thinking presented in Donald Moss's latest book is rich and complex, his clinical examples are interesting and apropos, and my review will surely not do it all justice. Between these covers Moss provides us an interesting collection of twelve papers loosely organized around two core themes: (1) the dangers of believing too strongly in “common sense” notions of reality (generally deemed “obvious”) and (2) the work of thinking. The latter requires delayed gratification and/or a willingness to tolerate states of unknowing relative to mysteries yet to be solved, despite commonsense explanations suggesting they have been. Moss believes that psychoanalysis stands squarely in opposition to commonsense notions of reality, though he astutely observes that the field creates commonsense answers of its own that can seriously stifle the analyst's efforts to find effective interventions/interpretations that are specific enough to a patient's situation and that don't sound like they've been drawn from dusty textbooks housing our collective wisdom. Best to keep thinking erotic, advises Moss, and he goes to great lengths in this volume to outline what's required to achieve that end: “being brought to the edge of what one knows” (p. 48); “being exposed to tremendous temptation, bearing that temptation—in an important sense, feeling it—while nonetheless delaying gratification, perhaps permanently. A silence that bears this strain is on the way to becoming ‘erotic thought’” (p. 49).

Moss notes that common sense can be seductively persuasive, not only because it is sincerely offered but also because it provides one a sense of certainty that allays the epistemological anxiety of not knowing.

Freud: An Intellectual Biography. By Joel Whitebook. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2017, xii + 484 pp., $39.99 hardcover.

Lois Oppenheim

Lois Oppenheim

181 East 73rd Street, #10F
New York, NY 10021

Lois Oppenheim   University Distinguished Scholar and Professor, Montclair State University; Scholar Associate Member, New York Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Honorary Member, William Alanson White Psychoanalytic Society.

“Does the world need another biography of Sigmund Freud?” The question, posed by Joel Whitebook at the start of his “intellectual biography” devoted to the founder of psychoanalysis, is apt. For, indeed, Ernest Jones, Peter Gay, and more recently Adam Phillips and Elisabeth Roudinesco have already famously and extensively profiled the life, thought, and legacy of Freud. Whitebook's answer is, most fortunately, “an emphatic yes” in that current advances in research and theory, and (often unfavorable) assessments of psychoanalysis as well, allow him “to sort out important unanswered questions concerning Freud's life and address critical issues in contemporary psychoanalysis and philosophy” (p. 1). And what a sorting out it is!

One has only to enter “intellectual biography” into any internet search engine to see not only the number of volumes devoted to individuals who have left their mark on the thought of an era and beyond, but just how this kind of historical writing, a genre unto itself, is meant to unfold. Identifying the sociocultural context in which a particular contribution is deemed highly significant, tracing the life and work of the subject, analyzing the interrelation between them, and putting into high relief the historical implications of both—these are the rules of this formidable game. Numerous scholars have taken up the task and from Saint Augustine to Lenin, via Leibniz, Descartes, and Hume, from Karl Marx to Simone Weil and Richard Hofstadter, such biographies abound. Yet, while Whitebook delves deeply into the life of Freud and its relation to his monumental achievements, as well as the relevance of time and place to Freud's theorizing, his objectives also lie elsewhere.

interdisciplinary, psychoanalytic education, curriculum design, psychoanalytic curriculum, psychoanalytic theory, neuropsychoanalysis, critical thinking

Curriculum development can play a role in preparing future psychoanalysts to participate in ongoing dialogue with colleagues in neighboring disciplines. Curriculum design can be used to encourage an interdisciplinary perspective that helps candidates think about psychoanalytic knowledge in the context of what is known in other disciplines about the functioning of mind and brain. It is possible to teach these complex matters in a way that students find accessible and useful. Exemplars taken from the curriculum designed and taught at the Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research are presented, as are problems encountered with this curriculum and the lessons that have been learned.

Diana Moga  

Just as Ralph Roughton challenged us in 2001 to reexamine our theoretical understanding not just of homosexuality but of all sexuality (Roughton 2002), our panel aimed to challenge the psychoanalytic community to reexamine outdated theories of gender and their impact on our patients and on candidate education. We used an episode of the award-winning TV show Transparent to delve deeper into our personal experience of gender, our reactions to the problematizing of the gender binary, and the possibilities that come with a richer experience of gender boundaries.

The panel was arranged in response to a request from APsaA's Program Committee to its Committee on Gender and Sexuality, which Susan McNamara and I co-chair, for a panel on transgender issues. It was held at the Chicago meeting of APsaA in June 2016 and was chaired by Ethan Grumbach. Robert Galatzer-Levy and Susan McNamara were presenters. I was asked to be the panel's reporter and to speak about my perspective as a candidate. The panel takes us on a journey through the political, societal, and institutional forces that shape the gender binary, the violence engendered by counterphobic attempts to police this boundary, unconscious fantasies of the chaos that ensues if we loosen our mental grip on this boundary as portrayed by the series Transparent, and finally the creative potential of accepting the complex nature of gender boundaries, elegantly depicted as a fractal boundary by Galatzer-Levy.

In his introductory remarks to the panel, Grumbach dedicated the panel to the forty-nine people who died and the fifty-three who were injured at the Pulse Club mass shooting in Orlando earlier that same month.

Panel held at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Chicago, June 1916. Panelists: Ethan Grumbach (chair), Robert Galatzer-Levy, Susan McNamara.

Janice Lieberman

55 East 87th Street, Apt. 2J

Janice Lieberman   Training and Supervising Analyst and faculty, Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research, New York; member, IPA Committee on Sexual and Gender Diversity Studies.

It is a pleasure to review this book by the Italian psychoanalyst Riccardo Lombardi. He is in many ways larger than life, a true Renaissance man, thoroughly knowledgeable about psychoanalysis, Greek mythology, poetry, music, good food, and wine. (Indeed he is, in addition to his work as a psychoanalyst, a wine critic.) Lombardi is someone whose very presence is curative. He integrates bodily pleasures with the intellect and brings the essence of the body and its feelings into the clinical situation. He gets to the heart of matters, to the concrete real body. His cases demonstrate an application of Bion's theory in practice, so sorely needed by those of us struggling to understand Bion.

Lombardi thoroughly reviews the literature pertaining to the “body” after Freud's famous declaration that “the ego is first and foremost a bodily ego” (1923, p. 26). Seven of the ten chapters are previously published. The translation of the book, included in Adrienne Harris and Lewis Aron's Relational Perspectives Book Series, is excellent. Lombardi is a medically trained psychiatrist who, having originally planned to follow in his surgeon father's footsteps, learned about the important role of touch by watching his father operate. He describes how he is able to reach the most severe of psychotic patients by encouraging them to verbalize, to symbolize their inner bodily sensations, which have been cut off from the mind. He demonstrates how these concrete sensations can become experienced in the analyst's own body.

Disillusioning Gender

Britt-Marie Schiller

Britt-Marie Schiller

8820 Ladue Road, 3rd floor
St. Louis, MO 63124
E-mail: schillbm@webster.edu

Britt-Marie Schiller  

illusions, femininity, masculinity, transgender, complementarity, social structures, labial framework, negation as limit

Illusions are not errors but erroneous beliefs motivated by wishful ideas and fantasies. To disillusion gender is to challenge the traditional Freudian construction that splits masculinity and femininity into agency versus passivity, the first with power, the second without. Disillusioning femininity as impotent frees up potency and power as generativity. Disillusioning masculinity as phallic and omnipotent opens the masculine subject to permeability and vulnerability. Illusions regarding the transgender include the idea that there are only two gender categories and the idea that gender identity is generated solely from an internal sense of self. The wish “to be seen as” or “to pass as” one gender or the other shows that social structures exceed the individual. At least for now, the disillusionment of gender with which we are left marks a tension between the internal sense of gender identity and the social structures of gender.

Reconstruction in the Present Two-Person Psychoanalysis: The Wolf Man Case Reconstructed

Harold Blum

Harold Blum

23 The Hemlocks
Roslyn Estates, NY 11576
E-mail: hpblum1@gmail.com

Harold Blum  

We practice in an era of psychoanalytic diversity, occasioning concerns about confusion and diffusion, but also reevaluation and creative change. New ideas regarding the two-person analytic process, preoedipal development, self- and object relations, narcissism, and inquiry into analytic outcome have influenced the ongoing evolution of psychoanalytic thought. Transference and countertransference are now understood as complex compromise formations with interpersonal/intersubjective as well as traditional intrapsychic determinants (Racker 1968). The techniques of genetic interpretation and reconstruction were influenced from outside traditional/classical psychoanalysis by the concept of the analyst as “participant observer” (Sullivan 1953) and from within traditional analysis by Loewald (1960) and others who considered the analyst a real and new object. Transference and countertransference may to a degree be co-created compromise formations. However, there are limits to dyadic co-creation, in my opinion, since each person brings to treatment a unique personality that is not entirely malleable. Adult character, in particular, is relatively stable, enduring, and resistant to change (Baudry 1989). Although technical approaches to genetic interpretation and reconstruction have been modified over time, both remain important to clinical work and analytic theory. Even if the analyst does not engage in explicit reconstruction, it is an implicit aspect of the traditional analyst's clinical work. Reconstruction is prominent in Freud's case of the Wolf Man (Sergei Pankejeff, 1886-1979)

When Fathering Fails: Violence, Narcissism, and the Father Function in Ancient Tales and Clinical Analysis

Michael Diamond

Michael Diamond

615 Hanley Way
Los Angeles, CA 90049
E-mail: michael@drmichaeljdiamond.com

Michael Diamond  

paternal function, triangular space, symbolic father, dead father, narcissistic father, murdered father, Oedipus, Antigone, Isaac, Freud, Lacan, Perelberg

Manifestations of failures in both symbolic and actual flesh-and-blood fathering reveal the inescapable role played by the father's unconscious transmissions in the ever present triadic matrix. Perelberg's crucial distinction between the murdered narcissistic father and the dead symbolic father suggests the problematic internalizations that fail to uphold the paternal law required for oedipal resolution. Three father-child narratives derived from classical literature and mythology are presented in order to elucidate significant unconscious paternal fantasies pertaining to lethal rivalry and neglect of the child's otherness. These narratives suggest how the father's insufficient management of rivalrous, envious, and murderously aggressive impulses—as well as narcissistic needs that fail to take into account the child's alterity—combine with the child's intrapsychic propensities to constrict triangular space and restrict development. Consequently, unavoidable father-child rivalries, absences, neglect, and desires often make it vital to recover the “missing” paternal function in analytic space. A clinical example illustrates the impact of this absence on the analyst's countertransference.

Twos and Threes: Musical Chairs in Female Psychic and Social Life

Nancy Kulish and Deanna Holtzman

Deanna Holtzman

625 Purdy Street
Birmingham, MI, 48009
E-mail: nkulish@aol.com

Deanna Holtzman  

Persephone complex, sibling oedipal triangle

Jockeying for position in the context of a threesome is a major preoccupation in female social behavior, and in female inner experience, throughout the life cycle. This oscillating phenomenon can be thought of as “twos and threes.” While such configurations are often understood in terms of sibling rivalry or social influences, the focus here is on underlying female triangular dynamics. “Twos and threes” are differentiated from the more familiar rivalries among siblings and from the concept of sibling oedipal triangles. Clinical examples and a contemporary novel by Tana French are presented to demonstrate that concealed and overlooked female oedipal or persephonal conflicts may underlie these experiences and appear in the transference and countertransference.

The Correspondence Between Sigmund Freud And Eugen Bleuler, 1904-1937: Introduction To Maetzener And Tessier

Gail Reed

Gail Reed

Gail Reed  

The foreign language section of JAPA Review of Books is dedicated to keeping readers informed about what is happening in psychoanalytic thought and practice in countries where a language other than English is the natural first choice in the treatment of patients, in discussing clinical material, and in thinking and writing about theory. Thus, when it comes to our attention that an author we are not familiar with is writing interesting things about psychoanalysis in Spanish, Italian, French, or German, or in another language, we review that writer's books in hopes of extending his or her audience to the anglophone world.

But what can we do when we are faced with a robust but incomplete piece of psychoanalytic history? In this case, the piece of history is a partial correspondence in German between Freud and the psychiatrist Eugen Bleuler, director of the famed Burghölzli hospital in Zurich. What we do is two things: first, translate and/or describe the material of the historical event and, second, track its silent footprint in contemporary analytic life.

To do that, we needed two reviewers: Christian Maetzener of the New York Psychoanalytic Institute to narrate, translate, and describe the correspondence itself, followed by Hélène Tessier of Montreal, to bring a critical French eye to the correspondence, to the theoretical debates that ensued, and to their consequences in current organized psychoanalysis, as these become, in our reading, more and more apparent. Tessier began by reviewing the French translation of the correspondence, while Maetzener addressed the original German. We are extremely grateful to them for having been willing to work together.

Associate Book Review Editor for Foreign Books.

“Ich bin zuversichtlich, wir erobern bald die Psychiatrie”: Briefwechsel 1904-1937 (“I Am Confident We Soon Will Conquer Psychiatry”: Letters 1904-1937.) Edited by Michael Schröter. Basel: Schwabe Verlag, 2012, 287 pp., €40.50.

Christian Maetzener

Christian Maetzener

950 Park Avenue
New York, NY 10028
E-mail: maetzener@msn.com

Christian Maetzener   Training and Supervising Analyst and Dean of Education for Child Anaysis, New York Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Assistant Clinical Professor, Department of Psychiatry, Mount Sinai School of Medicine.

More than seventy-five years after the death of Eugen Bleuler (1857-1939), his correspondence with Sigmund Freud has been made available by his granddaughter Tina Joos-Bleuler in a volume edited by Michael Schröter, a Freud biographer and historian who has edited Freud's correspondence with others.1 Altogether, 79 letters from the years 1904-1937 have been found—26 by Freud and 53 by Bleuler.2 Freud's were handwritten in his typical way without corrections; Bleuler used a typewriter and made frequent typos, which he corrected and occasionally referred to as evidence of “complexes.” The letters have been published in their entirety and have been annotated with additional information; some have been reprinted in facsimile. The book also contains a foreword by Bleuler's granddaughter and an essay by Bernhard Küchenhoff, former director of the Burghölzli, the psychiatric university hospital in Zurich once headed by Bleuler, regarding the scientific exchange between the two men. Included as well are excerpts of Bleuler's diary from 1909 to 1913.

Despite many requests, Manfred Bleuler (1903-1994), the oldest son of Eugen Bleuler, withheld Freud's letters from publication during his lifetime.

Emotional Regulation in Women with Chronic Fatigue Syndrome and Depression: Internal Representations and Adaptive Defenses

Anthony Bram , Kiley Gottschalk and William Leeds

William Leeds

363 Massachusetts Avenue, Lower Level #1
Lexington, MA 02420
E-mail: anthony_bram@hms.harvard.edu

William Leeds  

Chronic fatigue syndrome (CFS) presents challenges in differential diagnosis and treatment. Complicating diagnosis is that its symptoms overlap with those of depression. This study applies psychoanalytic concepts to understand emotional regulation (ER) in women with CFS and/or depression. One hundred eighty-six women were assigned to four groups and compared: (a) CFS plus high er depression (CFS-HD); (b) CFS plus lower depression (CFS-LD); (c) depressive disorder (DD); and (d) healthy controls (HC). ER was operationalized by measures of capacity to form internal representations and adaptive defenses. The study's premise was that difficulties metabolizing emotions psychologically would be associated with their greater somatic expression. Some support was found for the hypothesis that CFS participants would exhibit more impairment in representing emotions and in adaptive defenses compared to the DD and HC groups, but this held only for the CFS-HD group. Although CFS-LD participants were expected to be more purely somatizing than the CFS-HD group, they instead showed more sophisticated capacities for ER than that group and recalled less distressing early relationships, revealing more resilience. Still, however, we found support for somatization in some CFS sufferers: Within both the CFS-HD and the CFS-LD groups, weaknesses in representing emotions and in defensive functioning were associated with more severe physical symptoms. Clinically, the heterogeneity of CFS and those who suffer from it indicates the need for individual assessment and depression treatment.

[[RunningHead]]

Boundaries and Representation in the Mexico-Texas Borderland: Reflections on Geography, Culture, and Identity

Ricardo Ainslie

Ricardo Ainslie

Austin, TX 78712-5800
E-mail: rainslie@austin.utexas.edu

Ricardo Ainslie  

The summer 2017 meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association, held in Austin, Texas, opened with a flourish: a town hall discussion titled “Race and Borders: Crisis in Contemporary American Identity.” Even twenty years ago it would have been unthinkable to frame the meetings with such an overtly political message for members to deliberate and discuss. That it was done now attests the fact that psychoanalysis (and APsaA with it) is growing and changing along with the world around it, a world in which issues of multiple social identities and cultural forces are increasingly being recognized as inescapable dimensions of life and, consequently, of our clinical work.

Psychoanalysis has long been criticized, not always fairly, for being indifferent to the way social reality impinges on psychic life. One is reminded of Bowlby's description, at a 1987 colloquium at the University of Virginia, of his early work with the child of a psychotic mother (see Bretherton 1992). Melanie Klein, who supervised the case, had been so singularly focused on the child's fantasy production that she was unable to see what Bowlby saw quite readily—that fluctuations in the mother's psychosis (which he witnessed in the waiting room) were reflected in the child's psychic functioning. This difference led eventually to Bowlby's ostracism from the psychoanalytic fold and the loss to the field of a brilliant mind.

M. K. Hage Centennial Professor in Education, Department of Educational Psychology, University of Texas at Austin.

Freud Bashers: Facts, Fictions, and Fallacies

Freud: The Making of an Illusion. By Frederick Crews. New York: Henry Holt, 2017, xx + 746 pp., $40.00 hardcover, $22.00 paperback.The Memory Wars: Freud's Legacy in Dispute. By Frederick Crews. New York: New York Review Books, 1995, xiv + 299 pp.

Henry Lothane

Henry Lothane

1435 Lexington Avenue
New York, NY 10128
E-mail: schreber@lothane.com

Henry Lothane   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai.

As W. H. Auden sang about Freud, “to us he is no more a person / now but a whole climate of opinion / under whom we conduct our different lives.” For Stefan Zweig, Freud “more than any other in our time deepened and broadened our knowledge of the soul of man”; he was a genius

in the class of seminal thinkers, like Aristotle, Kant, or Nietzsche; each has created a universe of meaning and a perennial legacy… . Since its appearance in the closing years of the 19th century, Freud's new psychology has been repeatedly discovered, repressed, and rediscovered, proof of his enduring legacy, whether confirmed or contested [Lothane 2006, pp. 285-286].

As a Freudian methodologist I use Freud's method of free association in practicing psychoanalytic psychotherapy (Lothane 2018a). As an historian of psychoanalysis I have shown that in his 1911 analysis of Schreber's Memoirs Freud misdescribed, misdiagnosed, and misinterpreted Schreber's life drama. Despite this, however, Schreber's book would inspire Freud to develop the theory of narcissism (Lothane 1992).

In the first decade of the twentieth century Freud was attacked by psychiatrists and defended by Eugen Bleuler (1910). Among Freud's friends were both recognized scholars (Henri Ellenberger 1970; Paul Roazen 1975) and hagiographic biographers like Kurt Eissler, for whom Freud could do no wrong.

[[RunningHead]]

“Creating a Selfish Bitch”: Between Narcissism and Object Relations

Sidney Phillips

Sidney Phillips

234 Church Street, Suite 300
New Haven, CT 06510-1802
E-mail: sidney.phillips@yale.edu

Sidney Phillips  

I plunge us into the middle of this ongoing analysis at a turning point because I think this moment in the clinical work illuminates the crux of the problem the analysand and I face. After describing these challenges, I will retrace the terrain he and I traversed to arrive at this point.

Two years into this thrice-weekly analysis using the couch, my analysand, a man in his early forties, said to me:

What type of complexity are you thinking of? I've been enjoying the independence and owning myself. I'm basking in it. I don't want to neglect myself. I gave too much to Mom, to George, to others. I don't have time for them currently. I'm more selfish. I need that. I'm not going to try to understand them more. I don't have time for that.

Later in this session, he added:

If it's real, it's a beautiful point you're making. I'm not sure what it means. It's okay it's simplistic and not complex for now. I don't have time for people's complexity now. You're creating a selfish bitch, it looks like. [laughs heartily]

It may seem ironic to regard my patient's characterizing himself as a “selfish bitch” as the product of creative analytic work, but it wouldn't if you knew of the harshly judgmental states of self-loathing he could fall into, states typical of the earlier phase of his analysis.

Associate Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Yale School of Medicine; faculty and Training and Supervising Analyst, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis.

Clinical plenary address, American Psychoanalytic Association, Chicago, June 23, 2018.

[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on Phillips

Donald Moss

Donald Moss

80 University Place
New York, NY 11201
E-mail: dbmossmd@gmail.com

Donald Moss  

A revered teacher of mine once made the audacious claim that all of clinical technique—its theorization and its practice—boils down to tact. The OED defines tact as “a ready and delicate sense of what is fitting and proper in dealing with others, so as to avoid giving offence, or win good will; skill or judgement in dealing with men or negotiating difficult or delicate situations; the faculty of saying or doing the right thing at the right time.”

I invite you to join me in treating Sidney Phillips's remarkable clinical work here as a systematically sustained effort at finding his way toward tactfulness, toward “saying or doing the right thing at the right time.”

A closer look at the word tact leads us to its sensuous companion, tactile. This places the word nearer the body and expands its meaning to include “a keen faculty of perception or discrimination likened to the sense of touch.”

“Likened to the sense of touch,” tact, psychoanalytic or otherwise, can be seen not only as a way of directly relating to a person, but also as an indirect way of relating to that person's body, to that person's skin—it becomes a “fitting and proper” manner of speech and behavior whose aim is to deftly, and multidimensionally, touch another person indirectly.

So, perhaps what we are looking for in proper clinical technique, and what Phillips has so adroitly found here, is a way of delicately touching our patients, finding a sensuously sound way to make contact—psychic skin to psychic skin—with our words and therapeutic approach. This way of positioning “tact,” and clinical technique, carries us back to Freud's prescient theorization of the ego as “first and foremost a bodily ego” (1923, p. 26). Touch the ego, then, and indirectly you are touching the body. The ego's sensitivity to verbal touch corresponds to the body's sensitivity to physical touch.

Unaffiliated with an institute; co-founder of Green Gang, a four-person collective working with climate change denial and the relation between the human and the natural worlds.

정신분석에서 얼마나 변화가 일어나야 하는가? 그 변화를 어떻게 이룰 수 있는가?

베스 스타인버그 and 최 건

최 건

최 건  

‘비아’(非我, non-self)와 교류를 못 하는 것이 바로 이 그림자 구역 (shadow zone)의 증상이다. 비아는 위험하고 불온한 것이라는 무의식적 두려움 때 문에 교류가 일어나지 못 한다…… 석화(石化, petrification) 라는 위험에 굴 복하지 않기 위해서 우리는 여러 격언(wise words)에 주의를 기울여야 한 다…… 격언 중 하나는, 우리의 수련에는 끝이 없다는 것이다. 그리고 또 하나의 격언으로 프로이트는 우리에게, 때때로 분석가의 카우치로 돌아 가서1 우리 자신의 무의식과 다시 친밀 (closeness)해지는 데 도움을 받을 필요가 있다는 생각을 남겼다.

-스테파노 볼로니니 Stefano Bolognini (2011, p. 11)

2015년 미국정신분석학회 동계 학술대회의 토요일 늦은 오후, 꽉 들어찬 관객 앞에서 이 야심만만한 표패널이 진행되었다. “정신분 석에서 얼마나 변화가 일어나야 하는가? 그 변화를 어떻게 이룰 수 있는가?”가 이 패널이 던진 질문이었다. 좌장 역할을 맡은 스탠리 코엔 Stanley Coen 이 패널을 소개했고, 뒤이어 피터 골드버그 Peter Goldberg가 임상 사례를 발표 했다. 토론자인 로즈마리 발삼 Rosemary Balsam, 주디스 야노프 Judith Yanof, 아이사 아바시 Aisha Abbasi와 리포터인 베스 스타인버그 Beth Steinberg가 골드버그의 사례와 그 사례가 제기한 문제들에 대해 코멘트했다. 사례 발표의 일부로 골드버그는 환자에 반응해서 가지게 된 자신의 생각과 느 낌, 그리고 치료 회기 동안의 모든 치료적 중재 하나하나를 왜 했 는지에 대한 생각을 설명하도록 요구 받았다. 골드버그의 생각, 그 리고 순간 순간 그가 환자와 어떻게 작업하는지를 패널과 청중 모 두가 경험할 수 있게 하는 것이 이 설명의 목적이었다. 이런 포맷 은, 경험 많은 분석가가 치료하는 장면을 참가자들이 들어 볼 수 있 도록 하기 위해 고안되었다. 즉, 참가자들이 치료의 과정을 밀착해 서 따라갈(closely follow)
